6.title: Free Markets
6.body: A kid wants to trade apples for another kid's oranges. Both kids want to swap. Should you let them?
choices: Let them trade. Both kids are happy.
Let them trade, but take one apple as a fee.
Tell them what they can trade.
Take the fruit and hand it out yourself.

6.title: Free Markets
6.body: A trader buys toys cheap in one town. He sells them in your town for a bit more. Some say that is unfair. What do you do?
choices: Let the trader sell. He moved the toys to where kids want them.
Let them sell, but cap how much they can earn.
Say they cannot charge more than they paid.
Have the town decide who can buy and sell.

6.title: Free Markets & Trade
6.body: Another town's toys are cheaper. Some say tax them to protect our toy maker. What do you do?
choices: Let people buy the cheaper toys.
Add a small tax on the toys.
Add a big tax to block them.
Ban toys from other towns.

6.title: Free Markets & Trade
6.body: Our town makes great bread. The next town makes great cloth. Some say we should make everything ourselves. What do you do?
choices: Trade bread for their cloth so both gain.
Trade a little. Make most things at home.
Stop trading and make it all here.
Have the town plan all trade.

6.title: Taxes & Spending
6.body: The town has some saved money. Do you spend it all, or keep taxes low?
choices: Keep taxes low. Spend only on what is needed.
Raise taxes a little for some new programs.
Raise taxes a lot for many programs.
Let the town control most of the money.

6.title: Taxes & Spending
6.body: Leaders want a big extra tax just on the richest families. What do you do?
choices: Keep one fair, low tax for everyone.
Add a small extra tax on the richest.
Add a big extra tax on the richest.
Let the town set each person's tax.

6.title: Taxes & Spending
6.body: The town wants a new stadium. Should taxes pay for it?
choices: Let private owners pay and build it.
Split the cost with taxes.
Pay for most of it with taxes.
Make taxpayers pay for all of it.

6.title: Taxes & Spending
6.body: Some want free town buses paid for by a new tax. What do you do?
choices: Charge a small fare so riders pay for it.
Mix small fares with some tax money.
Make it free with higher taxes.
Have the town run all travel.

6.title: Education
6.body: Some families do not like the only school. How do you help the kids?
choices: Let families pick the school they want.
Open a few new kinds of schools to try.
Just give the old school more money.
Make every school follow one strict plan.

6.title: Education
6.body: Some parents want to teach kids at home or in small groups. What do you do?
choices: Let families pick home or small-group learning.
Allow it, but add lots of forms and checks.
Push everyone to the main school.
Make all kids go to the same school.

6.title: Education & School Choice
6.body: Some families want a voucher to pick any school. What do you do?
choices: Give families the choice of school.
Allow it with lots of rules.
Keep kids in their set school.
Only one school is allowed.

6.title: Education & School Choice
6.body: Some want every school to teach the exact same lessons. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents pick what works.
Set a few rules but allow choices.
Make most lessons the same everywhere.
Force one exact plan on all schools.

6.title: Money
6.body: Prices keep going up. Saved money buys less. What do you do?
choices: Stop making extra money so prices calm down.
Make new money more slowly.
Tell shops to freeze their prices.
Print lots of money for everyone.

6.title: Money
6.body: The town wants a big party. Some say print new money to pay for it. What do you do?
choices: Pay with real saved money, or wait.
Print a little money just once.
Print all the money the party needs.
Print money a lot for fun events.

6.title: Money & Inflation
6.body: Bread is scarce. Some want a law to keep its price low. What do you do?
choices: Let prices move so stores restock.
Cap the price a little.
Force a very low price.
Have the town hand out all bread.

6.title: Money & Inflation
6.body: Prices are rising. Some want to freeze all prices for a year. What do you do?
choices: Let prices move and slow the money printing.
Freeze a few prices for a short time.
Freeze all prices for a year.
Have the town set every price.

6.title: Business & Rules
6.body: A kid wants to open a lemonade stand. But the rules take forever. What do you do?
choices: Make the rules quick and simple.
Keep the rules, but give a few kids money.
Add even more rules and forms.
Let the town run the only stand.

6.title: Business & Rules
6.body: To run a food cart, the town makes you pay a lot and wait months. What do you do?
choices: Make the food-cart permit cheap, fast, and safe.
Keep the permit, but skip it for a few carts.
Add more fees and classes.
Let only the town sell food outside.

6.title: Business & Regulation
6.body: New ride apps compete with taxis. Taxis want them banned. What do you do?
choices: Let both compete fairly.
Allow apps with heavy rules.
Limit how many apps can run.
Ban the ride apps.

6.title: Business & Regulation
6.body: Hair braiders must take a costly, year-long class to work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the rule so braiders can work freely.
Keep a short, cheap safety class.
Keep the long, costly class.
Let only a few people braid hair.

6.title: Jobs & Wages
6.body: Some workers earn low pay. Leaders want to force much higher pay. What do you do?
choices: Grow the town so bosses compete for workers.
Raise the lowest pay a small amount.
Force a big pay raise right now.
Let the town set everyone's pay.

6.title: Jobs & Wages
6.body: Leaders want to ban part-time and side jobs to 'protect' full-time work. What do you do?
choices: Let people pick part-time, side, or full-time work.
Allow side jobs, but add many rules.
Cut back side and part-time jobs a lot.
Allow only town-approved full-time jobs.

6.title: Jobs & Wages
6.body: Some want to raise the lowest wage a lot. What do you do?
choices: Let pay rise as skills and business grow.
Raise the wage a little.
Force a big wage jump.
Set all pay from town hall.

6.title: Jobs & Wages
6.body: Some want the town to give everyone a government job. What do you do?
choices: Help private businesses grow and hire.
Add a few town jobs plus private ones.
Make most jobs government jobs.
Have the town control all jobs.

6.title: Property & Land
6.body: A factory dumps trash in the river and hurts neighbors. What is fair?
choices: Make the factory pay for the harm it caused.
Write strict rules for how it must work.
Have the town run the factory.
Take the factory away from the owner.

6.title: Property & Land
6.body: The town wants to take some families' homes to build a mall. What do you do?
choices: Protect the homes. Build only on land people sell.
Take the homes, but pay the families.
Take the homes for the 'greater good.'
Take any land the town wants.

6.title: Property & Environment
6.body: A factory pollutes the river. What is the fair fix?
choices: Make the factory pay for the harm it causes.
Ask the factory to pollute less.
Let the factory pollute freely.
Have the town run the factory.

6.title: Property & Environment
6.body: A shared lake is being overfished. What is the fair fix?
choices: Give clear fishing rights so people do not overfish.
Ask everyone to fish a little less.
Let anyone take all they want.
Have the town take the whole lake.

6.title: Banking & Saving
6.body: Families want a safe place to save money. How should banks work?
choices: Let honest banks compete for savers.
Let banks make risky loans the town pays for.
Let the town run the only bank.
Control all money from the top.

6.title: Banking & Saving
6.body: Leaders want to cap how much interest banks can charge. What do you do?
choices: Let interest match the real risk of the loan.
Cap rates a little on some loans.
Cap all rates way down.
Have the town set every rate.

6.title: Banking & Saving
6.body: A risky bank is failing. Should the town bail it out?
choices: Let it fail. Protect savers' insured money.
Give it a small loan.
Bail it out fully.
Have the town run all banks.

6.title: Banking & Saving
6.body: Some want a new tax on the interest people earn from saving. What do you do?
choices: Keep saving untaxed so people build a cushion.
Tax savings interest a little.
Tax savings interest a lot.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.

6.title: Free Speech
6.body: A newspaper says town leaders made a mistake. Some want it shut down. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, even when it stings.
Make them get the article approved first.
Ban anything that makes leaders look bad.
Take over the newspaper.

6.title: Free Speech
6.body: A speaker some people dislike wants to talk in the town square. What do you do?
choices: Let them speak. People can listen or walk away.
Allow it, but check the topic first.
Ban speakers most people dislike.
Allow only town-approved speakers.

6.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
6.body: Some want a book removed from the library. What do you do?
choices: Keep the book. Let people choose what to read.
Keep it, but hide it away.
Remove books many dislike.
Only allow approved books.

6.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
6.body: Some want people to get a town permit before holding a march. What do you do?
choices: Let people gather and speak without a permit.
Require a quick, free notice for big crowds.
Require a permit officials can deny.
Allow only marches the town approves.

6.title: Health Care
6.body: Doctor visits cost a lot in town. How do you make care cheaper?
choices: Let clinics compete and show clear prices.
Keep things the same, but help pay the bills.
Add strict rules on what care is allowed.
Have the town run all care and set prices.

6.title: Health Care
6.body: Skilled nurses could do more basic care. But the rules say only doctors may. What do you do?
choices: Let trained nurses do more of what they can.
Loosen the rules a little.
Keep strict rules on who can help.
Have the town assign all care jobs.

6.title: Health Care
6.body: Medicine costs less in another town. Should people buy it there?
choices: Let people buy safe, cheaper medicine.
Allow it with heavy rules.
Block the cheaper medicine.
Have the town sell all medicine.

6.title: Health Care
6.body: A rule limits how many new clinics can open. A nurse wants to open one. What do you do?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete.
Allow a few new clinics with rules.
Keep the limit on new clinics.
Have the town run all clinics.

7.title: Free Markets
7.body: Your town and the next town each make goods the other wants. Some leaders want to tax goods that cross the border. What do you do?
choices: Let the towns trade freely, with no border tax.
Allow trade but charge a small import fee.
Put a big tax on imports to protect local sellers.
Ban outside goods and control all trade.

7.title: Free Markets
7.body: A trader buys goods cheaply in one town. Then he resells them in yours for more. Some call it unfair. What do you do?
choices: Let the trader sell. He moved goods to where people want them.
Let them sell but put a cap on their profit.
Forbid selling above the buying price.
Have the town control all buying and selling.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: A nearby town sells cheaper toys. Local shop owners want a tax on them. What do you do?
choices: Let families buy the cheaper toys.
Put a small tax on the toys.
Put a high tax to keep them out.
Ban toys made in other towns.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Our town is good at baking bread. The next town is good at making cloth. Some say we should make everything ourselves. What do you do?
choices: Trade bread for their cloth so both towns gain.
Trade a little but make most things at home.
Stop trading and make it all here.
Have officials plan all trade.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Leaders want to limit how many toys come in from other towns. What do you do?
choices: Let toys come in freely so prices stay low.
Allow a lot at first, then set a cap.
Set a tight limit on toys coming in.
Ban toys from other towns.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Some want a rule that the town can only buy from local sellers. What do you do?
choices: Buy from whoever offers the best deal.
Prefer local sellers a little.
Require buying local most of the time.
Ban buying from outside sellers.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Other towns want to buy our grain. Some say keep it all here. What do you do?
choices: Let farmers sell grain to whoever they want.
Allow sales to other towns, but limit them.
Keep most grain from leaving.
Ban selling grain to other towns.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Another town sells goods super cheap. Local sellers want them blocked. What do you do?
choices: Let people enjoy the cheaper goods.
Block them only sometimes.
Block the cheap goods to protect sellers.
Ban goods from that town.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Two towns each make something the other wants. A leader says stop buying from the other town. What do you do?
choices: Keep trading so both towns get more of what they need.
Trade, but add a small fee on the other town's goods.
Block most goods from the other town.
Let leaders control all trading.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Another town taxed our goods. Some want us to tax theirs back hard. What do you do?
choices: Keep our trade open even if they do not.
Add a small matching tax.
Tax their goods back hard.
Ban all trade with that town.

7.title: Free Markets & Trade
7.body: Some want only the town government allowed to trade with other places. What do you do?
choices: Let people and shops trade freely themselves.
Allow private trade plus a town trader.
Let mostly the town handle trade.
Make the town the only trader.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: The town has savings. Some want to spend it on new programs. Others want low taxes. Your call?
choices: Keep taxes low and spend only on key needs.
Raise taxes a bit for a few new programs.
Raise taxes a lot for many programs.
Let the town direct most spending.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: Leaders want a special extra tax only on the richest families. What do you do?
choices: Keep one fair, low tax that applies to everyone.
Add a small extra tax on the richest.
Add a large extra tax on the richest.
Let officials set each person's share.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: The town wants a new stadium. Should taxes pay for it?
choices: Let private owners pay for and build it.
Split the cost between taxes and owners.
Pay for most of it with taxes.
Pay for all of it with taxes.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: Some want free town buses paid for with a new tax. What do you do?
choices: Charge a small fare so riders pay for it.
Mix small fares with some tax money.
Make it free with higher taxes.
Have the town run all travel.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: A big factory asks the town for special tax money to stay. What do you do?
choices: Say no. Treat all businesses the same.
Give a small, short subsidy. A subsidy is money the town gives a business.
Give a large subsidy.
Have the town take over the factory.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: Some want much higher tax rates on people who earn more. What do you do?
choices: Use one low, simple rate for everyone.
Add slightly higher rates at the top.
Set steep rates on higher earners.
Let officials set each person's rate.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: Some want to tax most people to send everyone a check. What do you do?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn.
Send a small check paid for by some tax.
Tax a lot to send big checks.
Have the town hand out all income.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: The town wants a big new program paid for by borrowing lots. What do you do?
choices: Spend only what the town can afford now.
Borrow a little for it.
Borrow a lot for the program.
Borrow with no limit.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: The town wants a new project. You can pay from savings or raise taxes. What do you do?
choices: Pay from savings and keep taxes low.
Raise taxes a little to pay for it.
Raise taxes a lot for many new programs.
Take what the town needs from whoever has it.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: Some want a huge tax on a snack they dislike. What do you do?
choices: Let people choose. Share facts, not heavy taxes.
Add a small tax on it.
Put a huge tax on it.
Ban the snack.

7.title: Taxes & Spending
7.body: A new program pays so much that some stop working. What do you do?
choices: Help people while keeping work worth it.
Pay a lot with small work rules.
Pay so much that work does not pay.
Pay everyone whether they work or not.

7.title: Education
7.body: Families are unhappy with the only school. How do you give students the best shot?
choices: Let families choose among different schools.
Add a few charter or special schools.
Just give the school more money.
Force one strict plan on every school.

7.title: Education
7.body: Some parents want to homeschool or form small learning pods. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose homeschooling or pods.
Allow it but require heavy paperwork.
Steer everyone toward the main school.
Require all children to attend the same school.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some families want a voucher to pick any school. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose their school.
Allow it with many rules.
Keep kids in their assigned school.
Allow only the district school.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some want every school to follow the exact same set of lessons. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents choose what works.
Set a few standards but allow choices.
Make most lessons the same everywhere.
Force one exact plan on all schools.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some families want to start a charter school that runs differently. What do you do?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete.
Allow them, but with many rules.
Discourage charters to protect district schools.
Allow only the district school.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some want every school forced to give the same big test. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents judge progress their own way.
Use one light shared test.
Require heavy testing everywhere.
Tie all school choices to one test.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some want to ban private schools so all kids go to one system. What do you do?
choices: Let private schools exist beside public ones.
Allow them, but with heavy rules.
Discourage private schools.
Ban private schools.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: A skilled tutor without a state card wants to teach. What do you do?
choices: Let parents choose tutors they trust.
Allow it, but add some paperwork.
Require a costly state card to tutor.
Allow only state-approved teachers.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some families want to pick a different school for their kids. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose the school that fits their child.
Allow it, but only from a short approved list.
Make every kid go to the one assigned school.
Let the town decide what every child must learn.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Some families want a savings account to spend on tutors, books, or classes. What do you do?
choices: Let funds follow the child to what they need.
Allow it, but with strict limits.
Keep money only in one system.
Let officials decide every dollar.

7.title: Education & School Choice
7.body: Officials want to take over a private college. What do you do?
choices: Let private and public schools both exist.
Take it over, but keep some independence.
Run the college through the town.
Ban private colleges.

7.title: Money
7.body: Prices keep rising, and savings buy less. What do you do?
choices: Stop making extra money so prices settle.
Slow down new money while watching prices.
Order shops to freeze prices.
Print lots of money to hand out.

7.title: Money
7.body: Leaders suggest printing new money to pay for a big town festival. What do you do?
choices: Pay from real savings, or skip it this year.
Print a little money just this once.
Print whatever the festival needs.
Print money often for events.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Bread is scarce. Some want a law to keep its price low. What do you do?
choices: Let prices change so stores restock fast.
Set a small price limit.
Force a very low price.
Have the town ration all bread.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Prices are rising. Some want to freeze all prices for a year. What do you do?
choices: Let prices move and slow the money printing.
Freeze a few prices for a short time.
Freeze all prices for a year.
Have officials set every price.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Officials want to mix cheap metal into the coins to make more money. What do you do?
choices: Keep the coins honest and full-value.
Mix in a little cheap metal.
Mix in a lot of cheap metal.
Let the town change the coins anytime.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Politicians want to control money rules to win votes. What do you do?
choices: Keep money rules steady and free of politics.
Let politics nudge the rules a little.
Let politicians set money rules for votes.
Print and spend as leaders wish.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Some want to give everyone free cash by printing new money. What do you do?
choices: Pay only from real money the town has.
Print a little for small payments.
Print lots for big payments.
Print money for everyone forever.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Officials want to fix the money's value by law, even if it is wrong. What do you do?
choices: Let the money's value float freely.
Guide the value a little.
Fix the value by law.
Punish anyone who trades at other rates.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Prices keep going up and money buys less. What do you do?
choices: Keep the money supply steady and stable.
Print a little more money to spend now.
Print a lot of money to pay for programs.
Set the price of everything by law.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Officials want to ban cash and force one town digital money. What do you do?
choices: Let people use cash or apps as they like.
Push digital money, but allow some cash.
Force one digital money on all.
Track and control every payment.

7.title: Money & Inflation
7.body: Officials want to force interest rates very low to push cheap loans. What do you do?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and saving.
Nudge rates down a little.
Force rates far below the real level.
Set all rates from town hall.

7.title: Business & Rules
7.body: A young baker wants to open a shop. But permits take a year and cost a lot. What do you do?
choices: Cut the red tape so honest shops can open fast.
Keep the rules but give a few bakers a grant.
Add more inspections and paperwork.
Have the town run its own bakery.

7.title: Business & Rules
7.body: Running a food cart needs an expensive license that takes months. What do you do?
choices: Make the license cheap, fast, and focused on safety.
Keep the license but waive it for a few.
Add more fees and required classes.
Let only the town sell street food.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: Ride apps now compete with taxis. Taxis want them banned. What do you do?
choices: Let both compete fairly.
Allow apps with heavy rules.
Limit how many apps can run.
Ban the ride apps.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: Hair braiders must take a costly, year-long class before they can work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the rule so braiders can work freely.
Keep a short, cheap safety class.
Keep the long, costly class.
Let only a few people braid hair.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: A big store wants to open. Local shops want it blocked. What do you do?
choices: Let it open and compete fairly.
Allow it, but with heavy rules.
Block the big store to protect local shops.
Let the town pick which stores may open.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: Movers must get a hard, costly license to work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the barrier so movers can start working.
Keep a small, cheap safety check.
Keep the hard, costly license.
Let only a few movers be allowed.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: People must get a costly permit to run a business from home. What do you do?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely.
Require a quick, cheap sign-up.
Keep the costly permit.
Ban businesses run from home.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: Opening a small shop needs months of forms and permits. What do you do?
choices: Cut the red tape so shops open easily. Red tape means too many rules.
Keep a short, simple sign-up.
Keep the months of paperwork.
Let officials approve each shop.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: A neighbor wants to open a small bakery, but there are lots of forms and rules. What do you do?
choices: Keep the rules simple so it is easy to start.
Add a few extra rules just in case.
Require many permits and inspections.
Let the town run the bakery instead.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: After a storm, sellers raise prices on water. Some want a law to forbid it. What do you do?
choices: Let prices rise so more water gets shipped in.
Allow small raises only.
Forbid any price rise.
Have the town hand out all water.

7.title: Business & Regulation
7.body: A shop got big by being popular. Some want to break it up. What do you do?
choices: Let it keep winning as long as it competes fairly.
Add extra rules just for being big.
Break up the shop for being big.
Have the town run the big shop.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some workers earn low pay. Leaders want to force much higher pay. What do you do?
choices: Grow the economy so employers compete for workers.
Raise the minimum wage a small amount.
Force a big raise right away.
Let the government set everyone's pay.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some leaders want to limit part-time and gig work to protect full-time jobs. What do you do?
choices: Let people choose part-time, gig, or full-time work.
Allow flexible work but add many rules.
Sharply limit gig and part-time work.
Allow only approved full-time jobs.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want to raise the lowest wage a lot. What do you do?
choices: Let pay rise as skills and businesses grow.
Raise the wage a little.
Force a big wage jump.
Set every wage from town hall.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want the town to give everyone a government job. What do you do?
choices: Help private businesses grow and hire.
Add some town jobs alongside private ones.
Make most jobs government jobs.
Have the town control all jobs.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want every worker forced to join the union. What do you do?
choices: Let workers choose whether to join.
Allow unions, but with some pressure to join.
Require most workers to join.
Make the union control all jobs.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want to force every business to pay for long leave. What do you do?
choices: Let workers and bosses agree on benefits.
Require a small amount of paid leave.
Force a large paid-leave benefit.
Have the town set all benefits.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want a top limit on how much anyone can earn. What do you do?
choices: Let people earn as much as they fairly can.
Allow high pay, but tax the top hard.
Cap how much people can earn.
Have the town set everyone's pay.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: New machines could do some jobs. Some want them banned to save jobs. What do you do?
choices: Allow machines. Workers move to new jobs.
Allow machines, but slow them down.
Ban machines to save old jobs.
Have the town control all machines.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: A teen wants a first job to learn skills. Some want one high pay rule for all jobs. What do you do?
choices: Let workers and bosses agree on pay.
Set a small pay floor. A pay floor is the lowest legal wage.
Set a high pay floor for every job.
Have the town assign everyone a job.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want to force shops to hire by set quotas. What do you do?
choices: Let shops hire the best person for each job.
Set soft hiring targets.
Force strict hiring quotas. A quota is a required number.
Have officials assign all workers.

7.title: Jobs & Wages
7.body: Some want a law so businesses can never lay anyone off. What do you do?
choices: Let firms adjust staff as needs change.
Allow layoffs, but add big penalties.
Ban layoffs almost entirely.
Have the town control all hiring and firing.

7.title: Property & Land
7.body: A factory dumps waste in the river and harms its neighbors. What is fair?
choices: Make the factory pay for the harm it causes.
Write strict rules for how it must run.
Have the town run the factory.
Seize the factory and the land.

7.title: Property & Land
7.body: The town wants to take some families' homes to build a mall. What do you do?
choices: Protect homeowners. Build only on land people sell willingly.
Take the homes but pay the owners.
Take the homes for the 'greater good'.
Seize whatever land the town wants.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: A factory pollutes the river. What is the fair fix?
choices: Make the factory pay for the harm it causes.
Ask the factory to pollute less on its own.
Let the factory pollute as it likes.
Have the town take over the factory.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: A shared lake is being overfished. What is the fair fix?
choices: Give clear fishing rights so the lake is not drained.
Ask everyone to fish a bit less.
Let anyone take all they want.
Have the town seize the whole lake.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: Rents are high. Some want a law to freeze rents. What do you do?
choices: Let more homes get built to bring rents down.
Freeze rents a little. A freeze stops rents from rising.
Freeze rents hard.
Have the town own all housing.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: Rules stop people from building new homes on their own land. What do you do?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their land.
Allow building, but with some limits.
Keep strict rules against building.
Have the town decide all building.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: Some land sits empty. Officials want to seize it. What do you do?
choices: Leave it with its owner to use or sell.
Tax empty land a little.
Take land that sits empty.
Have the town take all empty land.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: A shared well is running low as everyone takes water. What do you do?
choices: Give clear water rights so people do not waste it.
Ask people to use a bit less.
Let anyone take all they want.
Have the town take the well.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: A family owns a pond. Some want the town to take it to protect it. What do you do?
choices: Let the owners keep and care for it.
Let them keep it, but add many use rules.
Limit how they can use their own land.
Take the pond for the town.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: Some want to force landowners to let the public walk through their yards. What do you do?
choices: Respect owners. Ask them to share if they wish.
Force access to a small strip.
Force public access to most land.
Let anyone use any land anytime.

7.title: Property & Environment
7.body: Factories pollute. Some want a hard ban, others a market in pollution permits. What do you do?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup goes where it is cheapest. Permits let firms buy and sell the right to pollute.
Set one fixed rule for every factory.
Ignore the pollution.
Have the town run every factory.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Families want a safe place to save and borrow. How should banking work?
choices: Let honest banks compete for savers.
Let banks make risky loans the town backs.
Have the town run the only bank.
Control all money and lending from the top.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Leaders want to cap how much interest banks can charge. What do you do?
choices: Let rates reflect the real risk of each loan.
Cap rates a little on some loans.
Cap all rates well below the market.
Have the town set every interest rate.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: A risky bank is failing. Should the town bail it out?
choices: Let it fail, but protect savers' insured money.
Give it a small emergency loan.
Bail it out fully.
Have the town run all banks.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Some want a new tax on the interest people earn from saving. What do you do?
choices: Keep saving untaxed so families can build a cushion.
Tax savings interest a little.
Tax savings interest a lot.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Officials want to force banks to make a set number of risky loans. What do you do?
choices: Let banks lend based on real risk.
Require a few forced loans.
Set big forced loan targets.
Have the town run all lending.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Some want only one town-run bank allowed. What do you do?
choices: Let many banks compete for savers.
Allow a few banks plus a town one.
Allow mostly the town bank.
Make the town bank the only one.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Banks charge fees. Some want a law to cap them low. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete to offer low fees.
Cap a few fees.
Set strict low fee caps.
Have the town set all bank fees.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Officials want to limit how much money people can take out of the bank. What do you do?
choices: Let people use their own money freely.
Limit big withdrawals only.
Cap how much people can take out.
Freeze people's accounts.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: You want people to save money for the future. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete and keep saving simple.
Promise to cover every risk with town money.
Tax the interest people earn from saving.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Some want a fee on money people keep in savings to make them spend. What do you do?
choices: Let people save freely without a penalty.
Add a small fee on big savings.
Charge a fee on most savings.
Take a share of idle savings.

7.title: Banking & Saving
7.body: Some want a rule that banks may only lend inside our town. What do you do?
choices: Let banks lend where it makes the most sense.
Prefer local loans a little.
Require almost all loans stay local.
Have the town direct all loans.

7.title: Free Speech
7.body: A newspaper criticizes town leaders. Some want it shut down. What do you do?
choices: Protect the right to speak, even when it criticizes leaders.
Allow it but require approval first.
Ban articles that make leaders look bad.
Take over the press and control the news.

7.title: Free Speech
7.body: A controversial speaker wants to use the town square. Some want the speaker banned. What do you do?
choices: Allow the speech. People can listen, argue, or walk away.
Allow it but approve the topic first.
Ban speakers most people disagree with.
Allow only town-approved speakers.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want a book removed from the library. What do you do?
choices: Keep the book. Let people choose what to read.
Keep it but move it out of sight.
Remove books many people dislike.
Allow only approved books.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want people to get a town permit before holding a march. What do you do?
choices: Let people gather and speak without a permit.
Require a quick, free notice for big crowds.
Require a permit officials can deny.
Allow only marches the town approves.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want newspapers to get a town license to print. What do you do?
choices: Let anyone publish without a license.
Require a simple sign-up to publish.
Require a license officials can deny.
Allow only town-approved papers.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want everyone forced to say a town message they may not believe. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, or stay silent, freely.
Ask for the message, but allow opt-outs.
Require most people to say it.
Punish people who will not say it.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want to ban people from criticizing town leaders. What do you do?
choices: Let people criticize leaders freely.
Allow it, but punish rude words.
Ban harsh criticism of leaders.
Punish anyone who speaks against leaders.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want town officials to approve posts before people share them online. What do you do?
choices: Let people post without asking first.
Allow posts, but review some first.
Require approval before posting.
Allow only approved messages online.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: People say things you disagree with in the town square. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, even if you disagree.
Allow speech, but require a permit first.
Ban topics the town finds upsetting.
Only let the town's message be shared.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want to ban shops from advertising their prices and deals. What do you do?
choices: Let shops tell people about their goods.
Allow ads, but limit them a lot.
Ban most ads.
Let only town-approved ads run.

7.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
7.body: Some want the town to pick which charities may ask for donations. What do you do?
choices: Let any honest charity ask for support.
Allow it, but with heavy sign-up rules.
Let only approved charities collect.
Ban charities the town dislikes.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Health care is getting expensive. How do you make it affordable?
choices: Let providers compete and show clear prices.
Keep the system but add subsidies.
Tighten rules and limit treatments.
Have the government run all care and set prices.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Skilled nurses could do more basic care. But the rules save that work for doctors only. What do you do?
choices: Let trained nurses do more of what they are qualified to do.
Loosen the rules a little for some tasks.
Keep strict limits on who can treat patients.
Have the government assign all care tasks.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Medicine costs less in a nearby town. Should people buy it there?
choices: Let people buy safe, cheaper medicine.
Allow it with heavy rules.
Block the cheaper medicine.
Have the town be the only seller.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: A rule limits how many new clinics can open. A nurse wants to open one. What do you do?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete.
Allow a few new clinics with rules.
Keep the limit on new clinics.
Have the town run all clinics.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Medicine costs a lot. Some want the town to set low prices by law. What do you do?
choices: Let more sellers compete to lower prices.
Cap a few medicine prices. A cap is a legal limit.
Set strict low prices on medicine.
Have the town make all medicine.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Some want to force everyone to buy one health plan. What do you do?
choices: Let people pick the plan that fits them.
Require a basic plan with some choice.
Force everyone into the same plan.
Have the town provide the only plan.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Some want one government office to be the only place for care. What do you do?
choices: Let many clinics and doctors compete.
Allow private care plus a big public office.
Make most care go through one office.
Make the government the only provider.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: A charity clinic offers cheap care. The board wants to shut it. What do you do?
choices: Let the clinic keep helping patients.
Allow it, but add heavy rules.
Limit the clinic to protect hospitals.
Shut the charity clinic.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: People want good, affordable care. What do you do?
choices: Let doctors and patients choose, and let clinics compete.
Add rules that set some prices.
Have the town run most clinics.
Force every doctor to follow town orders.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Some want to ban private clinics so all care is public. What do you do?
choices: Let private and public care exist together.
Allow private care, but with heavy rules.
Discourage private clinics.
Ban private clinics.

7.title: Health Care
7.body: Some want to force doctors to accept only government-set fees. What do you do?
choices: Let doctors and patients agree on fees.
Set fees, but allow some exceptions.
Force one fee schedule on all doctors.
Have the town employ all doctors.

8.title: Free Markets
8.body: Two nearby towns each make something the other wants. Some leaders want to tax goods that cross the border. What should your town do?
choices: Let the towns trade freely with no border tax.
Allow trade, but add a small fee on imports.
Tax imports heavily to protect local sellers.
Ban outside goods and let the town control all trade.

8.title: Free Markets
8.body: A new shop wants to sell cheaper shoes than the old shop in town. The old shop asks you to stop the newcomer. What do you do?
choices: Let both shops compete for customers.
Let them compete, but give the old shop a subsidy.
Set one fixed price that both shops must charge.
Have the town run the only shoe store.

8.title: Free Markets
8.body: A trader buys goods cheaply in one town and sells them in yours for more. Some call it unfair. What do you do?
choices: Allow it. The trader took a risk and moved goods to where they are wanted.
Allow it, but cap how much profit they can make.
Ban reselling for more than the buying price.
Have the town control who can buy and sell.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: A nearby town sells toys more cheaply. Some shop owners want a tariff, a tax on imports, to protect local makers. What do you do?
choices: Allow the cheaper imports so families pay less.
Add a small tariff on the imports.
Set a high tariff to block them.
Ban goods from other towns.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Our town makes bread well, and the next town makes cloth well. Some argue we should produce everything ourselves. What do you do?
choices: Trade bread for their cloth so both towns gain.
Trade a little, but make most things at home.
Stop trading and produce it all locally.
Have officials plan all trade.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Leaders want to limit how many toys come in from other towns. What do you do?
choices: Let toys come in freely so prices stay low.
Allow a lot at first, then set a cap.
Set a tight limit on toys coming in.
Ban toys from other towns.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Some want a rule that the town can only buy from local sellers. What do you do?
choices: Buy from whoever offers the best deal.
Prefer local sellers a little.
Require buying local most of the time.
Ban buying from outside sellers.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Other towns want to buy our grain. Some say keep it all here. What do you do?
choices: Let farmers sell grain to whoever they want.
Allow sales to other towns, but limit them.
Keep most grain from leaving.
Ban selling grain to other towns.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Another town sells goods super cheap. Local sellers want them blocked. What do you do?
choices: Let people enjoy the cheaper goods.
Block them only sometimes.
Block the cheap goods to protect sellers.
Ban goods from that town.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Two towns each make something the other wants. A leader says stop buying from the other town. What do you do?
choices: Keep trading so both towns get more of what they need.
Trade, but add a small fee on the other town's goods.
Block most goods from the other town.
Let leaders control all trading.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Another town taxed our goods. Some want us to tax theirs back hard. What do you do?
choices: Keep our trade open even if they do not.
Add a small matching tax.
Tax their goods back hard.
Ban all trade with that town.

8.title: Free Markets & Trade
8.body: Some want only the town government allowed to trade with other places. What do you do?
choices: Let people and shops trade freely themselves.
Allow private trade plus a town trader.
Let mostly the town handle trade.
Make the town the only trader.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: Your town has money saved up. Some leaders want to spend it all on new programs. Others want to keep taxes low. What is your plan?
choices: Keep taxes low and spend carefully on core needs.
Raise taxes a little to fund several new programs.
Raise taxes a lot to fund many programs.
Have the government take over and direct most spending.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: The town wants to build something new. Leaders argue about how to pay for it. What do you choose?
choices: Fund only what the town can afford without new debt.
Borrow a moderate amount and pay it back slowly.
Borrow heavily now and worry about it later.
Print or seize money to pay for it.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: Leaders propose a special extra tax only on the town's richest families. What do you do?
choices: Keep one fair, low tax rule that applies broadly.
Add a small extra tax on the very richest.
Add a large extra tax on the richest.
Have the town decide each person's share.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: The town wants a new stadium. Should public taxes fund it?
choices: Let private owners finance and build it.
Share the cost between taxes and owners.
Cover most of the cost with taxes.
Fund the whole stadium with taxes.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: Some propose free town buses funded by a new tax. What do you do?
choices: Charge a modest fare so riders fund the service.
Combine modest fares with some tax support.
Make it free by raising taxes.
Have the town run all transportation.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: A big factory asks the town for special tax money to stay. What do you do?
choices: Say no. Treat all businesses the same.
Give a small, short subsidy. A subsidy is money the town gives a business.
Give a large subsidy.
Have the town take over the factory.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: Some want much higher tax rates on people who earn more. What do you do?
choices: Use one low, simple rate for everyone.
Add slightly higher rates at the top.
Set steep rates on higher earners.
Let officials set each person's rate.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: Some want to tax most people to send everyone a check. What do you do?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn.
Send a small check paid for by some tax.
Tax a lot to send big checks.
Have the town hand out all income.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: The town wants a big new program paid for by borrowing lots. What do you do?
choices: Spend only what the town can afford now.
Borrow a little for it.
Borrow a lot for the program.
Borrow with no limit.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: The town wants a new project. You can pay from savings or raise taxes. What do you do?
choices: Pay from savings and keep taxes low.
Raise taxes a little to pay for it.
Raise taxes a lot for many new programs.
Take what the town needs from whoever has it.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: Some want a huge tax on a snack they dislike. What do you do?
choices: Let people choose. Share facts, not heavy taxes.
Add a small tax on it.
Put a huge tax on it.
Ban the snack.

8.title: Taxes & Spending
8.body: A new program pays so much that some stop working. What do you do?
choices: Help people while keeping work worth it.
Pay a lot with small work rules.
Pay so much that work does not pay.
Pay everyone whether they work or not.

8.title: Education
8.body: Some families are unhappy with the only school in town. What policy gives students the best shot?
choices: Let families choose among different schools.
Add charter or specialty schools inside the system.
Just give the existing school much more money.
Force every school to follow one strict government plan.

8.title: Education
8.body: A talented student wants a program your town's school does not offer. What do you do?
choices: Let the student use funds at a school that offers it.
Ask the school to add the program if it can.
Tell the student to take what is offered.
Have the government assign all students and programs.

8.title: Education
8.body: A group of parents wants to teach their kids at home or in small learning pods. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose home or pod learning if they want.
Allow it, but require heavy paperwork and check-ins.
Discourage it and steer everyone to the main school.
Ban it so all children attend the same school.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some families want a voucher, public money they can use at any school, to choose where their kids go. What do you do?
choices: Give families the freedom to choose a school.
Allow vouchers under many restrictions.
Keep students in their assigned school.
Permit only the district school.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some want every school to follow one identical set of lessons. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents choose what works.
Set a few standards, but allow flexibility.
Make most lessons the same across schools.
Force one identical plan on all schools.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some families want to start a charter school that runs differently. What do you do?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete.
Allow them, but with many rules.
Discourage charters to protect district schools.
Allow only the district school.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some want every school forced to give the same big test. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents judge progress their own way.
Use one light shared test.
Require heavy testing everywhere.
Tie all school choices to one test.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some want to ban private schools so all kids go to one system. What do you do?
choices: Let private schools exist beside public ones.
Allow them, but with heavy rules.
Discourage private schools.
Ban private schools.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: A skilled tutor without a state card wants to teach. What do you do?
choices: Let parents choose tutors they trust.
Allow it, but add some paperwork.
Require a costly state card to tutor.
Allow only state-approved teachers.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some families want to pick a different school for their kids. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose the school that fits their child.
Allow it, but only from a short approved list.
Make every kid go to the one assigned school.
Let the town decide what every child must learn.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Some families want a savings account to spend on tutors, books, or classes. What do you do?
choices: Let funds follow the child to what they need.
Allow it, but with strict limits.
Keep money only in one system.
Let officials decide every dollar.

8.title: Education & School Choice
8.body: Officials want to take over a private college. What do you do?
choices: Let private and public schools both exist.
Take it over, but keep some independence.
Run the college through the town.
Ban private colleges.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Prices in town keep rising fast. People's savings buy less each month. What is your move?
choices: Stop creating extra money so prices can settle.
Create money more slowly while watching prices.
Order shops to freeze their prices.
Print lots of money to hand out to everyone.

8.title: Money
8.body: The town wants a big festival, and some leaders suggest printing new money to pay for it. What do you do?
choices: Pay for it from real savings, or skip it this year.
Print a little new money just this once.
Print whatever the festival needs.
Print money often to fund fun events.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Bread is scarce, and some want a price ceiling, a legal limit on the price, to keep it cheap. What do you do?
choices: Let prices adjust so sellers restock quickly.
Set a modest price ceiling.
Impose a strict, very low ceiling.
Have the town ration all bread.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Prices are climbing. Some want to freeze all prices for a year. What do you do?
choices: Let prices adjust and slow the money supply.
Freeze a few prices briefly.
Freeze all prices for a year.
Have officials set every price.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Officials want to mix cheap metal into the coins to make more money. What do you do?
choices: Keep the coins honest and full-value.
Mix in a little cheap metal.
Mix in a lot of cheap metal.
Let the town change the coins anytime.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Politicians want to control money rules to win votes. What do you do?
choices: Keep money rules steady and free of politics.
Let politics nudge the rules a little.
Let politicians set money rules for votes.
Print and spend as leaders wish.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Some want to give everyone free cash by printing new money. What do you do?
choices: Pay only from real money the town has.
Print a little for small payments.
Print lots for big payments.
Print money for everyone forever.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Officials want to fix the money's value by law, even if it is wrong. What do you do?
choices: Let the money's value float freely.
Guide the value a little.
Fix the value by law.
Punish anyone who trades at other rates.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Prices keep going up and money buys less. What do you do?
choices: Keep the money supply steady and stable.
Print a little more money to spend now.
Print a lot of money to pay for programs.
Set the price of everything by law.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Officials want to ban cash and force one town digital money. What do you do?
choices: Let people use cash or apps as they like.
Push digital money, but allow some cash.
Force one digital money on all.
Track and control every payment.

8.title: Money & Inflation
8.body: Officials want to force interest rates very low to push cheap loans. What do you do?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and saving.
Nudge rates down a little.
Force rates far below the real level.
Set all rates from town hall.

8.title: Business & Rules
8.body: A young baker wants to open a shop, but the permits take a year and cost a lot. What do you do?
choices: Cut the red tape so honest businesses can open fast.
Keep the rules, but offer a grant to a few bakers.
Add even more inspections and paperwork.
Have the town open its own bakery instead.

8.title: Business & Rules
8.body: To run a food cart, the town requires an expensive license that takes months. What do you do?
choices: Make the food-cart license cheap, quick, and focused on safety.
Keep the license, but waive it for a few carts.
Add more fees and required classes.
Only let the town sell food in public spaces.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: Ride-share apps now compete with taxis, and the taxi companies want them banned. What do you do?
choices: Let both compete on a level field.
Allow apps under heavy regulations.
Cap how many apps may operate.
Ban the ride-share apps.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: Hair braiders must complete a costly, year-long license to work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the requirement so braiders can work freely.
Keep a short, low-cost safety course.
Keep the long, expensive license.
Permit only a few licensed braiders.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: A big store wants to open. Local shops want it blocked. What do you do?
choices: Let it open and compete fairly.
Allow it, but with heavy rules.
Block the big store to protect local shops.
Let the town pick which stores may open.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: Movers must get a hard, costly license to work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the barrier so movers can start working.
Keep a small, cheap safety check.
Keep the hard, costly license.
Let only a few movers be allowed.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: People must get a costly permit to run a business from home. What do you do?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely.
Require a quick, cheap sign-up.
Keep the costly permit.
Ban businesses run from home.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: Opening a small shop needs months of forms and permits. What do you do?
choices: Cut the red tape so shops open easily. Red tape means too many rules.
Keep a short, simple sign-up.
Keep the months of paperwork.
Let officials approve each shop.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: A neighbor wants to open a small bakery, but there are lots of forms and rules. What do you do?
choices: Keep the rules simple so it is easy to start.
Add a few extra rules just in case.
Require many permits and inspections.
Let the town run the bakery instead.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: After a storm, sellers raise prices on water. Some want a law to forbid it. What do you do?
choices: Let prices rise so more water gets shipped in.
Allow small raises only.
Forbid any price rise.
Have the town hand out all water.

8.title: Business & Regulation
8.body: A shop got big by being popular. Some want to break it up. What do you do?
choices: Let it keep winning as long as it competes fairly.
Add extra rules just for being big.
Break up the shop for being big.
Have the town run the big shop.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some workers in town earn low wages. Leaders want to force a much higher minimum wage. What do you do?
choices: Grow the economy so employers compete for workers.
Raise the minimum wage a small, careful amount.
Force a large minimum wage increase right away.
Have the government set everyone's pay.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some leaders want to ban part-time and gig work to 'protect' full-time jobs. What do you do?
choices: Let people choose part-time, gig, or full-time work.
Allow flexible work, but add lots of rules.
Limit gig and part-time work sharply.
Allow only government-approved full-time jobs.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some propose a large increase in the minimum wage. What do you do?
choices: Let wages rise as skills and businesses grow.
Raise the minimum modestly.
Force a steep wage increase.
Have officials set every wage.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some propose that the town guarantee everyone a government job. What do you do?
choices: Help private businesses grow and hire.
Add some public jobs alongside private ones.
Make most employment government jobs.
Have the town direct all employment.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some want every worker forced to join the union. What do you do?
choices: Let workers choose whether to join.
Allow unions, but with some pressure to join.
Require most workers to join.
Make the union control all jobs.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some want to force every business to pay for long leave. What do you do?
choices: Let workers and bosses agree on benefits.
Require a small amount of paid leave.
Force a large paid-leave benefit.
Have the town set all benefits.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some want a top limit on how much anyone can earn. What do you do?
choices: Let people earn as much as they fairly can.
Allow high pay, but tax the top hard.
Cap how much people can earn.
Have the town set everyone's pay.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: New machines could do some jobs. Some want them banned to save jobs. What do you do?
choices: Allow machines. Workers move to new jobs.
Allow machines, but slow them down.
Ban machines to save old jobs.
Have the town control all machines.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: A teen wants a first job to learn skills. Some want one high pay rule for all jobs. What do you do?
choices: Let workers and bosses agree on pay.
Set a small pay floor. A pay floor is the lowest legal wage.
Set a high pay floor for every job.
Have the town assign everyone a job.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some want to force shops to hire by set quotas. What do you do?
choices: Let shops hire the best person for each job.
Set soft hiring targets.
Force strict hiring quotas. A quota is a required number.
Have officials assign all workers.

8.title: Jobs & Wages
8.body: Some want a law so businesses can never lay anyone off. What do you do?
choices: Let firms adjust staff as needs change.
Allow layoffs, but add big penalties.
Ban layoffs almost entirely.
Have the town control all hiring and firing.

8.title: Property & Land
8.body: A factory is dumping waste into the town river, hurting neighbors downstream. What is the fair fix?
choices: Hold the factory responsible for the harm it causes.
Write detailed rules for exactly how to operate.
Have the government run the factory itself.
Seize the factory and the land around it.

8.title: Property & Land
8.body: The town wants to take some families' homes to build a shopping center. What do you do?
choices: Protect homeowners. Build only on land that is sold willingly.
Take the homes, but pay the owners.
Take the homes for the 'greater good'.
Seize whatever land the town wants.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: A factory is polluting the river. What is the fair fix?
choices: Make the factory pay for the harm it causes.
Ask the factory to pollute less voluntarily.
Let the factory pollute without limit.
Have the town take over the factory.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: A shared lake is being overfished. What is the fair fix?
choices: Assign clear fishing rights so the lake is not drained.
Ask everyone to fish a bit less.
Let anyone take as much as they want.
Have the town seize the entire lake.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: Rents are high. Some want a law to freeze rents. What do you do?
choices: Let more homes get built to bring rents down.
Freeze rents a little. A freeze stops rents from rising.
Freeze rents hard.
Have the town own all housing.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: Rules stop people from building new homes on their own land. What do you do?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their land.
Allow building, but with some limits.
Keep strict rules against building.
Have the town decide all building.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: Some land sits empty. Officials want to seize it. What do you do?
choices: Leave it with its owner to use or sell.
Tax empty land a little.
Take land that sits empty.
Have the town take all empty land.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: A shared well is running low as everyone takes water. What do you do?
choices: Give clear water rights so people do not waste it.
Ask people to use a bit less.
Let anyone take all they want.
Have the town take the well.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: A family owns a pond. Some want the town to take it to protect it. What do you do?
choices: Let the owners keep and care for it.
Let them keep it, but add many use rules.
Limit how they can use their own land.
Take the pond for the town.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: Some want to force landowners to let the public walk through their yards. What do you do?
choices: Respect owners. Ask them to share if they wish.
Force access to a small strip.
Force public access to most land.
Let anyone use any land anytime.

8.title: Property & Environment
8.body: Factories pollute. Some want a hard ban, others a market in pollution permits. What do you do?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup goes where it is cheapest. Permits let firms buy and sell the right to pollute.
Set one fixed rule for every factory.
Ignore the pollution.
Have the town run every factory.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Families want a safe place to save and borrow. How should banking work in your town?
choices: Let trustworthy banks compete and be honest with savers.
Let banks operate, but guarantee every risky loan.
Have the government run the only bank.
Control all money and lending from the top.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Leaders want to cap how much interest banks can charge on loans. What do you do?
choices: Let interest rates reflect real risk and demand.
Cap rates a little for certain loans.
Cap all loan rates well below the market.
Have the town set every interest rate.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: A bank that made risky bets is failing. Should the town bail it out?
choices: Let it fail while protecting savers' insured deposits.
Extend a small emergency loan.
Bail the bank out in full.
Have the town run all the banks.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Some propose a new tax on the interest people earn from savings. What do you do?
choices: Keep savings untaxed so families build a cushion.
Tax the interest on savings a little.
Tax the interest on savings heavily.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Officials want to force banks to make a set number of risky loans. What do you do?
choices: Let banks lend based on real risk.
Require a few forced loans.
Set big forced loan targets.
Have the town run all lending.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Some want only one town-run bank allowed. What do you do?
choices: Let many banks compete for savers.
Allow a few banks plus a town one.
Allow mostly the town bank.
Make the town bank the only one.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Banks charge fees. Some want a law to cap them low. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete to offer low fees.
Cap a few fees.
Set strict low fee caps.
Have the town set all bank fees.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Officials want to limit how much money people can take out of the bank. What do you do?
choices: Let people use their own money freely.
Limit big withdrawals only.
Cap how much people can take out.
Freeze people's accounts.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: You want people to save money for the future. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete and keep saving simple.
Promise to cover every risk with town money.
Tax the interest people earn from saving.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Some want a fee on money people keep in savings to make them spend. What do you do?
choices: Let people save freely without a penalty.
Add a small fee on big savings.
Charge a fee on most savings.
Take a share of idle savings.

8.title: Banking & Saving
8.body: Some want a rule that banks may only lend inside our town. What do you do?
choices: Let banks lend where it makes the most sense.
Prefer local loans a little.
Require almost all loans stay local.
Have the town direct all loans.

8.title: Free Speech
8.body: A newspaper prints an article criticizing town leaders. Some want it shut down. What do you do?
choices: Protect the right to speak, even when it is critical.
Allow it, but require that articles be approved first.
Ban articles that make leaders look bad.
Take over the press and control the news.

8.title: Free Speech
8.body: A controversial speaker wants to talk in the town square. Some residents want them banned. What do you do?
choices: Allow the speech. Let people listen, argue, or walk away.
Allow it, but require approval of the topic first.
Ban speakers most people disagree with.
Allow only town-approved speakers.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some residents want a book removed from the public library. What do you do?
choices: Keep the book and let people choose what to read.
Keep it, but move it out of sight.
Remove books many people dislike.
Stock only officially approved books.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want residents to get a town permit before holding a march. What do you do?
choices: Let people assemble and speak without a permit.
Require a brief, free notice for large crowds.
Require a permit officials may deny.
Allow only marches the town approves.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want newspapers to get a town license to print. What do you do?
choices: Let anyone publish without a license.
Require a simple sign-up to publish.
Require a license officials can deny.
Allow only town-approved papers.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want everyone forced to say a town message they may not believe. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, or stay silent, freely.
Ask for the message, but allow opt-outs.
Require most people to say it.
Punish people who will not say it.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want to ban people from criticizing town leaders. What do you do?
choices: Let people criticize leaders freely.
Allow it, but punish rude words.
Ban harsh criticism of leaders.
Punish anyone who speaks against leaders.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want town officials to approve posts before people share them online. What do you do?
choices: Let people post without asking first.
Allow posts, but review some first.
Require approval before posting.
Allow only approved messages online.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: People say things you disagree with in the town square. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, even if you disagree.
Allow speech, but require a permit first.
Ban topics the town finds upsetting.
Only let the town's message be shared.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want to ban shops from advertising their prices and deals. What do you do?
choices: Let shops tell people about their goods.
Allow ads, but limit them a lot.
Ban most ads.
Let only town-approved ads run.

8.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
8.body: Some want the town to pick which charities may ask for donations. What do you do?
choices: Let any honest charity ask for support.
Allow it, but with heavy sign-up rules.
Let only approved charities collect.
Ban charities the town dislikes.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Care in town is getting expensive. People disagree on how to make it affordable. What do you choose?
choices: Let providers compete and let prices be clear.
Keep the system, but add government subsidies.
Tighten rules and limit which treatments are allowed.
Have the government run all care and set all prices.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Skilled nurses could handle more basic care, but strict rules say only doctors may. What do you do?
choices: Let trained nurses do more of what they are qualified for.
Loosen the rules a little for some tasks.
Keep strict limits on who can treat patients.
Have the government assign all care tasks.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Medicine is cheaper in a neighboring town. Should residents be allowed to buy it there?
choices: Allow people to buy safe, cheaper medicine.
Permit it under heavy restrictions.
Block the cheaper medicine.
Have the town be the only seller of medicine.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: A rule caps how many new clinics may open, and a nurse wants to start one. What do you do?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete.
Permit a few new clinics under rules.
Keep the cap on new clinics.
Have the town operate all clinics.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Medicine costs a lot. Some want the town to set low prices by law. What do you do?
choices: Let more sellers compete to lower prices.
Cap a few medicine prices. A cap is a legal limit.
Set strict low prices on medicine.
Have the town make all medicine.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Some want to force everyone to buy one health plan. What do you do?
choices: Let people pick the plan that fits them.
Require a basic plan with some choice.
Force everyone into the same plan.
Have the town provide the only plan.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Some want one government office to be the only place for care. What do you do?
choices: Let many clinics and doctors compete.
Allow private care plus a big public office.
Make most care go through one office.
Make the government the only provider.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: A charity clinic offers cheap care. The board wants to shut it. What do you do?
choices: Let the clinic keep helping patients.
Allow it, but add heavy rules.
Limit the clinic to protect hospitals.
Shut the charity clinic.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: People want good, affordable care. What do you do?
choices: Let doctors and patients choose, and let clinics compete.
Add rules that set some prices.
Have the town run most clinics.
Force every doctor to follow town orders.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Some want to ban private clinics so all care is public. What do you do?
choices: Let private and public care exist together.
Allow private care, but with heavy rules.
Discourage private clinics.
Ban private clinics.

8.title: Health Care
8.body: Some want to force doctors to accept only government-set fees. What do you do?
choices: Let doctors and patients agree on fees.
Set fees, but allow some exceptions.
Force one fee schedule on all doctors.
Have the town employ all doctors.

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: Local producers want you to tax goods from the next region so they face less competition. Shoppers and exporters warn this will raise prices and invite retaliation. What do you decide?
choices: Keep trade open so each region makes what it does best.
Allow trade, but add a small tax on imports.
Impose high import taxes to protect local firms.
Have the government run all trade.

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: A reseller buys goods cheaply in one market and sells them in yours at a markup. Critics call this unfair. How do you respond?
choices: Allow it, since resellers move goods to where they are valued and earn a return.
Allow it, but cap the markup.
Ban selling above the purchase price.
Have the government control buying and selling.

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: A nearby town sells cheaper toys here, and local makers want a protective tariff. How do you respond?
choices: Allow the imports so consumers pay less and gain more choices.
Add a modest tax on the imports.
Set a high tariff to keep the imports out.
Ban goods from outside the town.

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: Your town makes bread efficiently, while a neighbor makes cloth efficiently. Some push for full self-sufficiency. How do you respond?
choices: Specialize and trade so both towns come out ahead.
Trade a little, but make most goods at home.
Stop trading and produce everything locally.
Put all trade under central planning.

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: Officials propose a quota, which limits how many goods may be imported. How do you respond?
choices: Allow imports freely, so consumers benefit from lower prices
Permit a high quota that does not bind until imports get very large
Impose a tight limit on imports
Ban all imports from rival towns

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: Officials propose a rule that the town buy only from local sellers. How do you respond?
choices: Buy from whoever offers the best value
Give local sellers a modest preference
Require local purchasing in most cases
Ban all purchases from outside sellers

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: Other towns want to buy our grain, but some demand that it stay home. How do you respond?
choices: Let producers sell to any willing buyer
Permit exports, but cap the volume
Keep most grain from being exported
Ban grain exports outright

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: A rival region undercuts your local factories on price. Owners demand tariffs, while shoppers want low prices. What do you do?
choices: Keep trade open so shoppers get the best prices
Add a modest tariff to help the local factories
Raise steep tariffs to protect local jobs
Put all trade under government control

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: A neighboring town taxed our goods, and some urge a heavy tax in return. How do you respond?
choices: Keep our trade open no matter what tariffs they set
Impose a small matching tariff
Retaliate with heavy tariffs
Cut off all trade with that town

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: Some propose that only the government may trade with other places. How do you respond?
choices: Let people and businesses trade freely
Allow private trade alongside a public trader
Let the government handle most trade
Make the government the only trader

9.title: Free Markets & Trade
9.body: A neighboring town sells goods below cost, and local sellers demand a block. How do you respond?
choices: Let consumers enjoy the cheaper goods
Block them in some cases
Block the cheap goods to protect local sellers
Ban imports from that town

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: A budget surplus tempts leaders to launch new programs, while others want to keep taxes low. Each program adds costs every year. What is your budget plan?
choices: Keep taxes low and fund only essential services.
Raise a little more revenue for a few targeted programs.
Expand spending and taxes to fund broad programs.
Centralize control of most spending.

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: Legislators propose a steep tax aimed only at top earners. Advisers warn that the wealthy may relocate. What is your call?
choices: Keep low, broad taxes to keep investment and talent in town.
Add a modest extra tax on top earners.
Impose a steep, targeted tax.
Let officials set each taxpayer's burden.

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: Supporters want public money for a new stadium, but the payoff to taxpayers is unclear. How do you proceed?
choices: Require private investors to finance and build it.
Divide the cost between taxpayers and the owners.
Cover most of the cost with public money.
Fund the entire project with taxes.

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: Some people propose fare-free buses paid for by a new tax. How do you proceed?
choices: Charge modest fares so riders bear the cost.
Combine modest fares with a partial subsidy.
Offer fare-free service paid for by higher taxes.
Put all transportation under public control.

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: A large factory asks for a special subsidy to stay in town. How do you respond?
choices: Decline, and keep tax rules equal for every firm
Offer a small, temporary subsidy
Grant a large subsidy
Have the government take over the factory

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: Some propose steeply higher tax rates on people who earn more. How do you respond?
choices: Keep one low, broad tax rate for everyone
Add slightly higher rates at the top
Impose steep rates on top earners
Let officials set each person's rate

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: Some propose taxing most residents in order to send everyone an equal check. How do you respond?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn
Send a small transfer funded by some tax
Tax heavily to fund large transfers
Have the state distribute all income

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: A surplus tempts leaders to launch new programs, while others urge restraint. Each program brings ongoing costs. What do you do?
choices: Keep taxes low and fund only core needs
Add one modest program funded by a small tax
Launch several tax-funded programs
Direct most income through the government

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: Some propose a steep sin tax on a product they dislike. How do you respond?
choices: Inform people and let them choose for themselves
Add a small tax on it
Impose a steep tax on it
Ban the product

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: A new aid program pays so much that some people stop working. How do you respond?
choices: Aid people while keeping work worthwhile
Pay generously with only light work rules
Pay so much that working no longer pays
Pay everyone regardless of whether they work

9.title: Taxes & Spending
9.body: The town wants a large new program paid for by heavy borrowing. How do you respond?
choices: Spend within the town's current means
Borrow modestly to fund it
Borrow heavily to fund the program
Borrow without limit

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: The district's only schools keep falling short. People argue over more funding, more competition, or central control. Which reform do you choose?
choices: Let funding follow students to the schools they choose.
Expand charter and specialty schools.
Pour more money into the current system.
