Impose one standardized curriculum.

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Families want the option to homeschool or form learning co-ops. Officials debate how much to allow. What is your stance?
choices: Support educational choice and let families pick their own model.
Allow it, but under heavy reporting rules.
Steer families toward the district school.
Require everyone to attend the district school.

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Reformers propose vouchers that let funding follow each student to a chosen school. What is your position?
choices: Adopt portable vouchers so the funding follows the child.
Allow vouchers, but under many conditions.
Keep assigning students by their home address.
Permit only the assigned district school.

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Reformers propose one required curriculum for every school. What is your position?
choices: Let schools and parents choose what works for them.
Set minimal standards, but keep the flexibility.
Standardize most instruction across all schools.
Impose one identical curriculum everywhere.

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Families want to open a charter school that uses a different approach. How do you respond?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Limit charters in order to shield district schools
Permit only the assigned district school

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Some propose requiring one standardized test for all schools. How do you respond?
choices: Let schools and families measure progress in flexible ways
Adopt one light, shared assessment
Require heavy testing across all schools
Tie every decision to a single test

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Some propose banning private schools so that all students attend one system. How do you respond?
choices: Let private schools operate alongside public ones
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Discourage private schooling
Ban private schools

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Families ask for options beyond the assigned school, but officials prefer one system. What do you do?
choices: Fund the student so families can choose a school
Allow a few charter options
Keep everyone in the assigned school
Set one curriculum for every school by law

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Families want education savings accounts to pay for tutors, books, and classes. How do you respond?
choices: Let the funds follow the child to whatever they need
Allow them, but under strict limits
Keep funding locked inside one system
Have officials direct every dollar

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: Officials propose taking over a private college. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public schools exist side by side
Take it over, but leave it some independence
Run the college through the government
Ban private colleges

9.title: Education & School Choice
9.body: A skilled tutor without a state credential wants to teach. How do you respond?
choices: Let parents choose the tutors they trust
Allow it, but require paperwork
Require a costly credential to tutor
Permit only state-approved teachers

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Ongoing inflation is shrinking real wages and savings. Your advisers split between restraint, price controls, and printing more money. What is your response?
choices: Restrain money growth to steady prices.
Slow money growth gradually.
Impose price controls on key goods.
Expand the money supply broadly.

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Some officials propose printing money to pay for a civic festival. What is your response?
choices: Fund it from real revenue, or skip it.
Authorize a small, one-time printing of money.
Print whatever the festival needs.
Rely on regular money printing to fund events.

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: During a bread shortage, officials propose a price ceiling. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise so suppliers restock quickly.
Set a modest price ceiling.
Impose a strict ceiling far below the market price.
Have the government ration the supply.

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Facing rising prices, officials propose a year-long freeze on all prices. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices adjust and rein in money growth.
Freeze a narrow set of prices for a short time.
Impose a full year-long price freeze.
Set every price from the center.

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Officials propose debasing the coins, mixing in cheaper metal so they can mint more. How do you respond?
choices: Keep the currency sound and worth its full value
Allow slight debasement
Permit heavy debasement
Let officials change the currency whenever they want

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Politicians want direct control of monetary policy to boost their own popularity. How do you respond?
choices: Keep monetary rules stable and shielded from politics
Allow some political influence over the rules
Let politicians set policy to win votes
Print and spend however leaders choose

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Some propose giving everyone free cash paid for by printing new money. How do you respond?
choices: Fund payments only from real revenue
Print a little to cover modest payments
Print large sums to cover big payments
Fund permanent payments by printing

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Rising prices are shrinking wages and savings, and advisers are split over what to do. What do you do?
choices: Keep the money supply growing at a steady, predictable pace
Expand the money supply a little to spend now
Expand the money supply a lot to fund programs
Freeze prices by law

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Officials propose banning cash and requiring one digital currency. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use cash or apps, whichever they prefer
Promote digital payments, but keep some cash
Require one official digital currency
Monitor and control every payment

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Officials propose forcing interest rates far below market levels to push cheap credit. How do you respond?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and the amount people save
Nudge rates modestly lower
Force rates far below market levels
Set all rates by decree

9.title: Money & Inflation
9.body: Officials propose fixing the currency's value by decree. How do you respond?
choices: Let the currency's value float freely
Guide the value modestly
Fix the value by decree
Punish trade at any other rate

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: An entrepreneur faces a costly licensing process that takes a year. Existing firms call these barriers 'quality control.' How do you balance safety and easy entry?
choices: Streamline licensing while keeping the real safety rules.
Keep the rules, but grant a few exceptions.
Add more inspections and compliance steps.
Open a government-run business instead.

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: A food cart needs an expensive license that takes months to get. Reformers question whether it is even necessary. How do you proceed?
choices: Trim licensing down to genuine safety needs, which lowers entry costs.
Keep licensing, but waive it for a few.
Add fees and required coursework.
Create a town monopoly on street food.

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: Ride-share platforms compete with licensed taxis, and the taxis demand a ban. How do you proceed?
choices: Let both compete under the same rules.
Allow the platforms, but under heavy regulation.
Cap how many platforms may operate.
Ban ride-share platforms outright.

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: Hair braiders must complete a costly license that takes a year. How do you proceed?
choices: Repeal the requirement so braiders can work freely.
Keep a short, low-cost safety course.
Keep the long, expensive license.
Permit only a few licensed braiders.

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: A large chain wants to open, and local shops demand that it be blocked. How do you respond?
choices: Allow it to open and compete on a level playing field
Allow it, but under heavy regulation
Block the chain in order to protect local shops
Let officials decide which stores are allowed to open

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: Moving companies must get a difficult, costly license before they can operate. How do you respond?
choices: Remove the barrier so movers can start working
Keep a small, low-cost safety check
Keep the difficult, costly license
Permit only a limited number of movers

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: Residents must get a costly permit before they can run a home business. How do you respond?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely
Require a quick, low-cost registration
Keep the costly permit
Ban home-based businesses

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: Starting a small business requires costly permits and long waits. What do you do?
choices: Streamline the rules so starting a business is quick and cheap
Trim a few rules, but keep most of them
Keep the heavy permit process
Have the government approve every new business

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: After a storm, sellers raise water prices, and some propose an anti-gouging law. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise so more supply rushes in
Allow only small price increases
Forbid any price increase
Have the government ration all water

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: A firm grew large because it was popular, and some want it broken up. How do you respond?
choices: Let it keep competing as long as it plays fair
Add extra rules just because of its size
Break up the firm for being big
Have the government run the firm

9.title: Business & Regulation
9.body: Opening a small shop requires months of forms and permits. How do you respond?
choices: Cut the red tape so shops can open easily
Keep a brief, simple registration
Keep the lengthy paperwork
Let officials approve each shop

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Advocates want a steep minimum wage. Economists warn it could cut jobs for low-skill workers. What is your choice?
choices: Pursue growth so employers compete and raise wages on their own.
Raise the minimum wage modestly and watch what happens to jobs.
Mandate a steep wage floor right now.
Have the state set all pay.

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: To protect full-time jobs, some people propose restricting part-time and gig work. What is your decision?
choices: Preserve flexible work and the freedom to make your own contracts.
Allow flexible work, but under heavy restrictions.
Sharply cut back gig and part-time work.
Permit only approved full-time jobs.

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Advocates push for a large minimum-wage increase. How do you respond?
choices: Let wages rise with productivity and competition for workers.
Raise the minimum wage modestly.
Mandate a steep increase.
Have officials set all wages.

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some people propose that the town guarantee everyone a government job. How do you respond?
choices: Help private businesses grow and hire.
Add some public jobs alongside the private ones.
Make most jobs government jobs.
Have the town direct all employment.

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some propose requiring every worker to join the union. How do you respond?
choices: Let each worker decide freely whether to join
Permit unions, but pressure workers to join
Require membership for most workers
Give the union control over every job

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some propose forcing every business to provide extensive paid leave. How do you respond?
choices: Let workers and employers negotiate benefits themselves
Require a modest amount of paid leave
Require a generous paid-leave benefit
Have officials set all benefits

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some propose a hard cap on how much anyone is allowed to earn. How do you respond?
choices: Let people earn as much as they fairly can
Allow high pay, but tax the top heavily
Cap maximum earnings
Have officials set everyone's pay

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some push a high wage floor for all jobs in order to raise pay. What do you do?
choices: Let pay be set by agreement between workers and employers
Set a modest wage floor
Set a high wage floor everywhere
Have officials assign wages for every role

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some propose forcing employers to hire by fixed quotas. How do you respond?
choices: Let employers hire the best fit for each role
Set soft hiring targets
Impose strict hiring quotas
Have officials assign all workers

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: Some propose a law that bars businesses from laying anyone off. How do you respond?
choices: Let firms adjust their staffing as needs change
Allow layoffs, but impose heavy penalties
Ban nearly all layoffs
Have the government control all hiring and firing

9.title: Jobs & Wages
9.body: New machines could do some jobs, and some demand a ban to save those jobs. How do you respond?
choices: Allow the machines, and let workers shift into new roles
Allow machines, but slow how fast they are adopted
Ban the machines to protect old jobs
Have the state control all machines

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: An upstream plant's pollution harms residents downstream, a classic spillover cost. The options range from liability to a takeover. What is the principled fix?
choices: Enforce property rights so the polluter pays for the harm it causes.
Write detailed command-and-control rules.
Put the plant under public operation.
Seize the plant and the surrounding land.

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: The town wants to use eminent domain to take homes for a new development. How do you weigh it?
choices: Protect owners and build only on land that is sold willingly.
Take the homes, but pay the owners for them.
Take the property for the public benefit.
Seize property whenever the town chooses.

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: A factory's waste is contaminating a shared river. What is the fair remedy?
choices: Hold the factory liable for the damage it causes others.
Ask the factory to reduce its waste voluntarily.
Allow unlimited discharge into the river.
Put the factory under town ownership.

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: A shared lake is being overfished. What is the fair remedy?
choices: Assign clear fishing rights so the lake is not drained.
Ask everyone to fish a little less.
Let anyone take as much as they want.
Have the government seize the lake.

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: Rents are rising, and some propose rent control. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage more housing to be built, which eases rents
Impose mild rent control
Impose strict rent control
Have the government own all housing

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: Zoning rules stop owners from building new homes on their own land. How do you respond?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their property
Permit building under some limits
Keep strict limits on building
Have officials control all building

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: Some land sits unused, and officials propose seizing it. How do you respond?
choices: Leave it with its owner to use or sell
Add a tax on unused land
Seize land that sits idle
Have the state take all unused land

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: Officials want to seize underused land so they can manage it themselves. What do you do?
choices: Leave it with owners to use, sell, or conserve
Add a tax to push owners into using the land
Restrict how owners may use their land
Seize the land for public management

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: Some propose forcing landowners to allow public access across their property. How do you respond?
choices: Respect owners, and invite them to share voluntarily
Force access across a small strip
Force public access across most land
Let anyone use any land at any time

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: Factories pollute, and some want a blanket ban while others want a market in permits. How do you respond?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup happens where it costs the least
Apply one fixed rule to every factory
Just tolerate the pollution
Have the government run every factory

9.title: Property & Environment
9.body: A shared well is running dry as everyone draws from it. How do you respond?
choices: Assign clear water rights to prevent waste
Ask everyone to use a bit less
Let anyone draw all they want
Have the government seize the well

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Households want safe savings and access to credit. Leaders debate competition, blanket guarantees, or a state bank. What is your framework?
choices: Support competitive banks under clear, transparent rules.
Allow banks, but guarantee all of their risky loans.
Create a single state bank.
Centralize all credit decisions.

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Officials propose capping the interest rates on loans. Lenders warn this will ration credit. What is your framework?
choices: Let rates reflect risk so credit stays available.
Cap rates modestly on some loans.
Impose strict caps below the market rate.
Have the state set all interest rates.

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: A bank went broke on risky bets and now asks for a public rescue. How do you respond?
choices: Let it fail, but protect the insured depositors.
Offer a limited emergency loan.
Provide a full bailout.
Place all banks under state control.

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Some people propose a new tax on the interest people earn from savings. How do you respond?
choices: Keep savings untaxed so families can build a cushion.
Tax the interest on savings modestly.
Tax the interest on savings heavily.
Have the state hold everyone's savings.

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Officials propose requiring banks to make a set quota of risky loans. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend based on the real risk of each loan
Require a small quota of loans
Impose large lending quotas
Have the state direct all lending

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Some propose allowing only one government-run bank. How do you respond?
choices: Let many banks compete for customers
Permit a few private banks alongside a public one
Allow mostly the government bank
Make the government bank the only one

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Banks charge fees, and some propose a low legal cap on them. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks compete to offer lower fees
Cap a few fees
Impose strict fee caps
Have officials set all bank fees

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Households want safe savings and fair credit, and leaders are debating the rules. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete under clear, simple rules
Guarantee every deposit fully with public funds
Tax the interest people earn from saving
Have the state run all banking

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Some propose a fee on money held in savings in order to spur spending. How do you respond?
choices: Let people save freely without penalty
Add a small fee on large balances
Charge a fee on most savings
Seize a share of idle savings

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Some propose requiring banks to lend only within the town. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend wherever it makes the most sense
Give local loans a modest preference
Require nearly all loans to stay local
Have officials direct all lending

9.title: Banking & Saving
9.body: Officials propose limiting how much people may withdraw from banks. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use their own money freely
Limit only very large withdrawals
Cap withdrawals broadly
Freeze accounts entirely

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Critical news coverage leads officials to seek restrictions. The public's right to know clashes with the government's image. What is your response?
choices: Protect free expression and a free press, including dissent.
Allow publication, but review it first.
Ban coverage that discredits officials.
Put the press under state control.

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: A controversial speaker asks to use the public square, and some people demand a ban. How do you respond?
choices: Protect the speech and reject bans based on viewpoint.
Allow it, but approve the topic first.
Ban speakers the majority opposes.
Permit only approved speakers.

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: A group asks the library to remove a book from its shelves. How do you respond?
choices: Keep the book and let individuals decide what to read.
Keep it, but limit who can access it.
Remove titles that a vocal group dislikes.
Stock only officially approved works.

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some people want residents to get a permit before holding a march. How do you respond?
choices: Let people assemble and speak without a permit.
Require brief, free notice for large crowds.
Require a permit that officials may deny.
Allow only the marches the town approves.

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some propose requiring newspapers to get a license before they may publish. How do you respond?
choices: Let anyone publish without a license
Require a simple registration to publish
Require a license that officials may deny
Permit only papers the officials approve

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some propose requiring everyone to recite an official message. How do you respond?
choices: Let people speak or stay silent as they choose
Request the message, but allow people to opt out
Require most people to recite it
Penalize anyone who refuses

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some propose banning any criticism of town officials. How do you respond?
choices: Let people criticize officials freely
Allow it, but punish disrespectful speech
Ban harsh criticism of officials
Punish anyone who speaks against officials

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some want to require official approval before people can hold a rally. What do you do?
choices: Protect the right to gather and speak
Allow rallies, but require a permit each time
Approve only the rallies the town agrees with
Ban public gatherings that criticize leaders

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some propose banning shops from advertising their prices and deals. How do you respond?
choices: Let shops inform people about their goods
Allow ads, but restrict them heavily
Ban most advertising
Permit only officially approved ads

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some propose letting the town decide which charities may ask for donations. How do you respond?
choices: Let any honest charity seek support
Allow it, but impose heavy registration
Permit only approved charities to collect
Ban the charities the town disfavors

9.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
9.body: Some propose requiring officials to approve online posts before they are shared. How do you respond?
choices: Let people post without prior approval
Allow posts, but pre-screen some of them
Require approval before posting
Permit only approved messages online

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Rising costs are straining families. The proposals range from competition and subsidies to rationing and single-payer control. What is your approach?
choices: Enable competition among providers and clear, visible prices.
Keep the current system, but expand subsidies.
Tighten the rules and restrict treatments.
Establish single-payer control of care and prices.

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Qualified nurses could handle more routine care, but the rules reserve it for physicians. What is your decision?
choices: Broaden what qualified nurses are allowed to do.
Relax the rules for a few tasks.
Keep the restrictive rules on who can treat patients.
Have the government assign all clinical tasks.

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Medicine is much cheaper in a neighboring town, and some people want to block buying it there. How do you respond?
choices: Let residents buy safe, lower-cost medicine elsewhere.
Allow it, but under strict rules.
Block imports of the cheaper medicine.
Make the town the only seller of medicine.

9.title: Health Care
9.body: A certificate-of-need rule caps new clinics, and a nurse wants to open one. How do you respond?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete.
Permit a few new clinics under strict rules.
Keep the cap on new clinics.
Have the government operate all clinics.

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Medicine is expensive, and some propose legal price controls. How do you respond?
choices: Let more sellers compete, which brings prices down
Cap the prices of a few medicines
Set strict price controls on medicine
Have the government produce all medicine

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Some propose forcing everyone to buy the same single health plan. How do you respond?
choices: Let people choose the plan that fits them
Require a basic plan, but allow other choices
Force everyone into an identical plan
Have the government provide the only plan

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Some propose making one government office the only provider of care. How do you respond?
choices: Let many clinics and doctors compete
Allow private care alongside a large public office
Route most care through one office
Make the government the only provider

9.title: Health Care
9.body: People want affordable, quality care, and some want one public system to run it all. What do you do?
choices: Let clinics and doctors compete, with real choice for patients
Keep private care alongside a large public option
Route most care through one public system
Make the government the only provider

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Some propose banning private clinics so that all care is public. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public care exist side by side
Allow private care, but under heavy rules
Discourage private clinics
Ban private clinics

9.title: Health Care
9.body: Some propose forcing doctors to accept only government-set fees. How do you respond?
choices: Let doctors and patients agree on fees
Set fees, but allow some exceptions
Impose one fee schedule on all doctors
Have the government employ all doctors

9.title: Health Care
9.body: A charity clinic offers low-cost care, and the board moves to shut it down. How do you respond?
choices: Let the clinic keep serving patients
Allow it, but under heavy rules
Restrict the clinic in order to protect hospitals
Shut down the charity clinic

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: Local factories want you to tax imports from a rival region to block competition. But consumers and exporters warn that prices will climb and the rival may strike back. What do you decide?
choices: Keep trade open so each region can focus on what it does best.
Allow trade, but add a moderate tariff on imports.
Impose high tariffs to protect the firms that already exist.
Have the government run and manage all trade.

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: A trader buys surplus goods in a faraway market and resells them in your city at a markup. Some residents call it exploitation. How do you respond?
choices: Permit it. Reselling moves goods to where people value them most and rewards the trader's risk.
Permit it, but cap how large the markup can be.
Ban reselling anything above its original purchase price.
Put all buying and selling under government direction.

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: A neighboring market exports cheap toys, and local producers push for a protective tariff. How do you respond?
choices: Permit the imports so consumers gain lower prices and a wider selection.
Place a moderate tariff on the imports.
Impose a steep tariff to shut them out.
Ban all goods produced outside the area.

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: Your area makes bread efficiently, and a neighbor makes cloth efficiently. Some want to produce everything at home. How do you respond?
choices: Specialize and trade, because focusing on each area's strength benefits both.
Trade a little, but produce most goods locally.
Give up trade and produce everything at home.
Put all trade under central planning.

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: Officials propose a quota, which limits how many goods may be imported. How do you respond?
choices: Allow imports freely, so consumers benefit from lower prices
Permit a high quota that does not bind until imports get very large
Impose a tight limit on imports
Ban all imports from rival towns

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: Officials propose a rule that the town buy only from local sellers. How do you respond?
choices: Buy from whoever offers the best value
Give local sellers a modest preference
Require local purchasing in most cases
Ban all purchases from outside sellers

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: Other towns want to buy our grain, but some demand that it stay home. How do you respond?
choices: Let producers sell to any willing buyer
Permit exports, but cap the volume
Keep most grain from being exported
Ban grain exports outright

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: A rival region undercuts your local factories on price. Owners demand tariffs, while shoppers want low prices. What do you do?
choices: Keep trade open so shoppers get the best prices
Add a modest tariff to help the local factories
Raise steep tariffs to protect local jobs
Put all trade under government control

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: A neighboring town taxed our goods, and some urge a heavy tax in return. How do you respond?
choices: Keep our trade open no matter what tariffs they set
Impose a small matching tariff
Retaliate with heavy tariffs
Cut off all trade with that town

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: Some propose that only the government may trade with other places. How do you respond?
choices: Let people and businesses trade freely
Allow private trade alongside a public trader
Let the government handle most trade
Make the government the only trader

10.title: Free Markets & Trade
10.body: A neighboring town sells goods below cost, and local sellers demand a block. How do you respond?
choices: Let consumers enjoy the cheaper goods
Block them in some cases
Block the cheap goods to protect local sellers
Ban imports from that town

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: A budget surplus tempts leaders to launch new programs, while others push to keep taxes low. Every program creates lasting costs. What is your budget stance?
choices: Keep taxes low and fund only the essential functions of government.
Raise a modest amount of revenue to pay for a few targeted programs.
Expand taxes and spending to fund broad entitlement programs.
Put most of the economy's resources under central government control.

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: Lawmakers propose a steep tax on only the highest earners. Advisers warn that mobile capital may simply relocate. What is your approach?
choices: Keep taxes low and broad-based to hold on to investment and skilled workers.
Add a modest extra tax on top incomes.
Enact a steep tax aimed narrowly at the top.
Let officials decide each taxpayer's burden case by case.

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: Boosters want public money for a stadium, even though the return to taxpayers looks doubtful. How do you proceed?
choices: Require private money to finance and build it.
Split the cost between taxpayers and the owners.
Cover most of the cost with public money.
Pay for the entire project with taxes.

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: Boosters want fare-free buses paid for by a new tax. How do you proceed?
choices: Charge modest fares so the riders bear the cost.
Mix modest fares with a partial subsidy.
Provide fare-free service by raising taxes.
Put all transportation under public operation.

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: A large factory asks for a special subsidy to stay in town. How do you respond?
choices: Decline, and keep tax rules equal for every firm
Offer a small, temporary subsidy
Grant a large subsidy
Have the government take over the factory

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: Some propose steeply higher tax rates on people who earn more. How do you respond?
choices: Keep one low, broad tax rate for everyone
Add slightly higher rates at the top
Impose steep rates on top earners
Let officials set each person's rate

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: Some propose taxing most residents in order to send everyone an equal check. How do you respond?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn
Send a small transfer funded by some tax
Tax heavily to fund large transfers
Have the state distribute all income

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: A surplus tempts leaders to launch new programs, while others urge restraint. Each program brings ongoing costs. What do you do?
choices: Keep taxes low and fund only core needs
Add one modest program funded by a small tax
Launch several tax-funded programs
Direct most income through the government

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: Some propose a steep sin tax on a product they dislike. How do you respond?
choices: Inform people and let them choose for themselves
Add a small tax on it
Impose a steep tax on it
Ban the product

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: A new aid program pays so much that some people stop working. How do you respond?
choices: Aid people while keeping work worthwhile
Pay generously with only light work rules
Pay so much that working no longer pays
Pay everyone regardless of whether they work

10.title: Taxes & Spending
10.body: The town wants a large new program paid for by heavy borrowing. How do you respond?
choices: Spend within the town's current means
Borrow modestly to fund it
Borrow heavily to fund the program
Borrow without limit

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: The district's monopoly schools keep falling short. People argue over funding, competition, and central control. What is your policy?
choices: Make funding portable so families can choose their own schools.
Expand charter schools and other specialized public options.
Increase funding for the existing school system.
Impose one required curriculum on every school.

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Families ask for the freedom to homeschool or form learning co-ops. Officials debate the limits. Where do you stand?
choices: Affirm educational choice so families can pick the model that fits their children.
Allow it, but require families to meet demanding reporting rules.
Nudge families toward the district school they are assigned to.
Require every student to attend the assigned district school.

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Reformers propose vouchers that let funding follow students to the school they choose. What is your position?
choices: Enact portable vouchers so the funding follows the child.
Permit vouchers, but only under extensive conditions.
Keep assigning students to their district schools.
Allow students to attend only their assigned school.

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Reformers propose one required curriculum for every school. What is your position?
choices: Let schools and parents choose what actually works.
Set minimal standards while keeping room for flexibility.
Standardize most teaching across all schools.
Impose one identical curriculum on every school.

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Families want to open a charter school that uses a different approach. How do you respond?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Limit charters in order to shield district schools
Permit only the assigned district school

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Some propose requiring one standardized test for all schools. How do you respond?
choices: Let schools and families measure progress in flexible ways
Adopt one light, shared assessment
Require heavy testing across all schools
Tie every decision to a single test

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Some propose banning private schools so that all students attend one system. How do you respond?
choices: Let private schools operate alongside public ones
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Discourage private schooling
Ban private schools

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Families ask for options beyond the assigned school, but officials prefer one system. What do you do?
choices: Fund the student so families can choose a school
Allow a few charter options
Keep everyone in the assigned school
Set one curriculum for every school by law

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Families want education savings accounts to pay for tutors, books, and classes. How do you respond?
choices: Let the funds follow the child to whatever they need
Allow them, but under strict limits
Keep funding locked inside one system
Have officials direct every dollar

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: Officials propose taking over a private college. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public schools exist side by side
Take it over, but leave it some independence
Run the college through the government
Ban private colleges

10.title: Education & School Choice
10.body: A skilled tutor without a state credential wants to teach. How do you respond?
choices: Let parents choose the tutors they trust
Allow it, but require paperwork
Require a costly credential to tutor
Permit only state-approved teachers

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Ongoing inflation eats away at real wages and savings. Your advisers split among restraint, price controls, and expansion. What is your response?
choices: Slow the growth of the money supply to hold prices steady.
Slow the growth of the money supply gradually.
Impose price controls on key goods.
Expand the money supply and hand it out.

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Several officials propose printing new money to pay for a civic festival. How do you respond?
choices: Fund it from real revenue, or skip it altogether.
Authorize a small, one-time printing of money.
Print whatever amount the festival requires.
Rely on regular money printing to pay for events.

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Facing a bread shortage, officials propose a strict price ceiling. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise to their market level so suppliers restock the shelves.
Impose a moderate price ceiling.
Set a strict ceiling below the market price.
Have the government ration and hand out the bread.

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: With inflation dragging on, officials propose a year-long freeze on all prices. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices adjust and rein in the growth of the money supply.
Freeze a narrow set of prices for a short time.
Impose a full year-long freeze on all prices.
Have the government set every price.

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Officials propose debasing the coins, mixing in cheaper metal so they can mint more. How do you respond?
choices: Keep the currency sound and worth its full value
Allow slight debasement
Permit heavy debasement
Let officials change the currency whenever they want

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Politicians want direct control of monetary policy to boost their own popularity. How do you respond?
choices: Keep monetary rules stable and shielded from politics
Allow some political influence over the rules
Let politicians set policy to win votes
Print and spend however leaders choose

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Some propose giving everyone free cash paid for by printing new money. How do you respond?
choices: Fund payments only from real revenue
Print a little to cover modest payments
Print large sums to cover big payments
Fund permanent payments by printing

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Rising prices are shrinking wages and savings, and advisers are split over what to do. What do you do?
choices: Keep the money supply growing at a steady, predictable pace
Expand the money supply a little to spend now
Expand the money supply a lot to fund programs
Freeze prices by law

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Officials propose banning cash and requiring one digital currency. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use cash or apps, whichever they prefer
Promote digital payments, but keep some cash
Require one official digital currency
Monitor and control every payment

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Officials propose forcing interest rates far below market levels to push cheap credit. How do you respond?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and the amount people save
Nudge rates modestly lower
Force rates far below market levels
Set all rates by decree

10.title: Money & Inflation
10.body: Officials propose fixing the currency's value by decree. How do you respond?
choices: Let the currency's value float freely
Guide the value modestly
Fix the value by decree
Punish trade at any other rate

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: An entrepreneur faces a costly, year-long licensing process. Established firms defend it as a way to ensure quality. How do you balance safety with the freedom to enter?
choices: Simplify licensing while keeping the rules that protect real safety.
Keep the licensing system, but grant exemptions to a select few.
Increase inspections and compliance requirements.
Create a government-run business to do the work instead.

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Opening a food cart requires an expensive, months-long license. Reformers question whether it is even necessary. How do you proceed?
choices: Trim licensing down to real safety needs, which lowers the cost of getting started.
Keep the license, but waive it for a chosen few.
Add more fees and required coursework.
Give the town a monopoly over all street food.

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Ride-share apps compete with licensed taxis, and the taxi operators demand a ban. How do you proceed?
choices: Let both compete under the same neutral rules.
Allow the platforms, but under heavy regulation.
Cap how many platforms are allowed to operate.
Ban ride-share platforms altogether.

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Hair braiders must finish a costly, year-long licensing program. How do you proceed?
choices: Repeal the mandate so braiders can work freely.
Keep a brief, inexpensive safety course.
Keep the lengthy, expensive license.
License only a limited number of braiders.

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: A large chain wants to open, and local shops demand that it be blocked. How do you respond?
choices: Allow it to open and compete on a level playing field
Allow it, but under heavy regulation
Block the chain in order to protect local shops
Let officials decide which stores are allowed to open

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Moving companies must get a difficult, costly license before they can operate. How do you respond?
choices: Remove the barrier so movers can start working
Keep a small, low-cost safety check
Keep the difficult, costly license
Permit only a limited number of movers

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Residents must get a costly permit before they can run a home business. How do you respond?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely
Require a quick, low-cost registration
Keep the costly permit
Ban home-based businesses

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Starting a small business requires costly permits and long waits. What do you do?
choices: Streamline the rules so starting a business is quick and cheap
Trim a few rules, but keep most of them
Keep the heavy permit process
Have the government approve every new business

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: After a storm, sellers raise water prices, and some propose an anti-gouging law. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise so more supply rushes in
Allow only small price increases
Forbid any price increase
Have the government ration all water

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: A firm grew large because it was popular, and some want it broken up. How do you respond?
choices: Let it keep competing as long as it plays fair
Add extra rules just because of its size
Break up the firm for being big
Have the government run the firm

10.title: Business & Regulation
10.body: Opening a small shop requires months of forms and permits. How do you respond?
choices: Cut the red tape so shops can open easily
Keep a brief, simple registration
Keep the lengthy paperwork
Let officials approve each shop

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Advocates demand a steep minimum wage, while economists warn it will cost low-skill workers their jobs. What is your choice?
choices: Pursue economic growth so employers compete for workers and bid up wages.
Raise the minimum wage modestly and watch what happens to jobs.
Require a steep minimum wage right away.
Have the government set all wages.

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: To protect full-time jobs, some propose restricting part-time and gig work. What is your decision?
choices: Preserve flexible work and the freedom to make your own contracts.
Permit flexible work, but only under heavy restrictions.
Sharply cut back gig and part-time arrangements.
Allow only approved full-time positions.

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Advocates push for a large increase in the minimum wage. How do you respond?
choices: Let wages rise as productivity grows and employers compete for workers.
Raise the minimum wage moderately.
Require a steep increase in the minimum wage.
Have the government set every wage.

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Advocates propose a government guarantee of a job for everyone. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage private business so it can grow and hire more workers.
Add some public jobs alongside the private ones.
Make most jobs public.
Put all employment under state direction.

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Some propose requiring every worker to join the union. How do you respond?
choices: Let each worker decide freely whether to join
Permit unions, but pressure workers to join
Require membership for most workers
Give the union control over every job

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Some propose forcing every business to provide extensive paid leave. How do you respond?
choices: Let workers and employers negotiate benefits themselves
Require a modest amount of paid leave
Require a generous paid-leave benefit
Have officials set all benefits

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Some propose a hard cap on how much anyone is allowed to earn. How do you respond?
choices: Let people earn as much as they fairly can
Allow high pay, but tax the top heavily
Cap maximum earnings
Have officials set everyone's pay

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Some push a high wage floor for all jobs in order to raise pay. What do you do?
choices: Let pay be set by agreement between workers and employers
Set a modest wage floor
Set a high wage floor everywhere
Have officials assign wages for every role

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Some propose forcing employers to hire by fixed quotas. How do you respond?
choices: Let employers hire the best fit for each role
Set soft hiring targets
Impose strict hiring quotas
Have officials assign all workers

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: Some propose a law that bars businesses from laying anyone off. How do you respond?
choices: Let firms adjust their staffing as needs change
Allow layoffs, but impose heavy penalties
Ban nearly all layoffs
Have the government control all hiring and firing

10.title: Jobs & Wages
10.body: New machines could do some jobs, and some demand a ban to save those jobs. How do you respond?
choices: Allow the machines, and let workers shift into new roles
Allow machines, but slow how fast they are adopted
Ban the machines to protect old jobs
Have the state control all machines

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: An upstream plant's waste harms residents downstream, a classic spillover cost. Fixes range from holding it liable to a public takeover. What is the principled choice?
choices: Enforce property rights so the polluter pays for the harm it causes.
Write detailed government rules that dictate exactly how firms must operate.
Transfer the plant to public operation.
Seize the plant and its land outright.

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: The town wants to use eminent domain to take homes for a private development. How do you weigh the proposal?
choices: Protect the owners and build only on land that is sold voluntarily.
Take the homes, but pay the owners compensation.
Seize property in the name of public benefit.
Seize property whenever the town alone decides to.

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: A factory's waste is polluting a shared river. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Hold the factory liable for the harm it inflicts on others.
Ask the factory to cut its pollution voluntarily.
Allow the factory to discharge waste without limit.
Bring the factory under city ownership.

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: A lake that everyone shares is being overfished. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Assign clear fishing rights to prevent the lake from being drained.
Ask fishers to cut their catch voluntarily.
Let anyone take as much as they want.
Bring the lake under public ownership.

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: Rents are rising, and some propose rent control. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage more housing to be built, which eases rents
Impose mild rent control
Impose strict rent control
Have the government own all housing

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: Zoning rules stop owners from building new homes on their own land. How do you respond?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their property
Permit building under some limits
Keep strict limits on building
Have officials control all building

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: Some land sits unused, and officials propose seizing it. How do you respond?
choices: Leave it with its owner to use or sell
Add a tax on unused land
Seize land that sits idle
Have the state take all unused land

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: Officials want to seize underused land so they can manage it themselves. What do you do?
choices: Leave it with owners to use, sell, or conserve
Add a tax to push owners into using the land
Restrict how owners may use their land
Seize the land for public management

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: Some propose forcing landowners to allow public access across their property. How do you respond?
choices: Respect owners, and invite them to share voluntarily
Force access across a small strip
Force public access across most land
Let anyone use any land at any time

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: Factories pollute, and some want a blanket ban while others want a market in permits. How do you respond?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup happens where it costs the least
Apply one fixed rule to every factory
Just tolerate the pollution
Have the government run every factory

10.title: Property & Environment
10.body: A shared well is running dry as everyone draws from it. How do you respond?
choices: Assign clear water rights to prevent waste
Ask everyone to use a bit less
Let anyone draw all they want
Have the government seize the well

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Households want safe savings and access to credit. Leaders debate competition, blanket guarantees, and state banking. What is your framework?
choices: Encourage competitive banking under clear, transparent rules.
Allow private banking, but have the government guarantee all risky lending.
Set up a single state-owned bank with a monopoly.
Centralize all control over money and credit in the government.

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Officials propose capping the interest rates lenders may charge. Lenders warn this will ration credit. What is your framework?
choices: Let interest rates reflect risk so credit stays available to many borrowers.
Cap rates modestly on certain kinds of loans.
Impose strict caps well below market rates.
Have the government set every interest rate.

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: A bank that gambled itself into insolvency asks for a public rescue. How do you respond?
choices: Let the bank fail while protecting its insured depositors.
Provide a limited emergency line of support.
Provide a full bailout.
Bring the entire banking sector under state control.

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Officials propose a new tax on the interest people earn from savings. How do you respond?
choices: Leave savings untaxed so households can build a financial cushion.
Tax the interest on savings modestly.
Tax the interest on savings heavily.
Place all savings in the custody of the state.

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Officials propose requiring banks to make a set quota of risky loans. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend based on the real risk of each loan
Require a small quota of loans
Impose large lending quotas
Have the state direct all lending

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Some propose allowing only one government-run bank. How do you respond?
choices: Let many banks compete for customers
Permit a few private banks alongside a public one
Allow mostly the government bank
Make the government bank the only one

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Banks charge fees, and some propose a low legal cap on them. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks compete to offer lower fees
Cap a few fees
Impose strict fee caps
Have officials set all bank fees

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Households want safe savings and fair credit, and leaders are debating the rules. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete under clear, simple rules
Guarantee every deposit fully with public funds
Tax the interest people earn from saving
Have the state run all banking

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Some propose a fee on money held in savings in order to spur spending. How do you respond?
choices: Let people save freely without penalty
Add a small fee on large balances
Charge a fee on most savings
Seize a share of idle savings

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Some propose requiring banks to lend only within the town. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend wherever it makes the most sense
Give local loans a modest preference
Require nearly all loans to stay local
Have officials direct all lending

10.title: Banking & Saving
10.body: Officials propose limiting how much people may withdraw from banks. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use their own money freely
Limit only very large withdrawals
Cap withdrawals broadly
Freeze accounts entirely

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Critical news coverage leads officials to seek restrictions, setting the public's right to know against the government's reputation. What is your response?
choices: Protect free expression and a free press, including the right to dissent.
Allow publication, but only after a government review beforehand.
Ban any coverage that makes officials look bad.
Put the press under direct state control.

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: A controversial speaker asks to use the public square, and some residents demand a ban. How do you respond?
choices: Protect the speech and reject bans based on the speaker's viewpoint.
Allow it, but require the topic to be approved in advance.
Ban speakers whom the majority opposes.
Permit only officially approved speakers.

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: A group petitions the library to remove a book. How do you respond?
choices: Keep the book and leave the reading choice to individuals.
Keep it, but restrict who can access it.
Remove titles that a vocal group opposes.
Stock only works that officials have approved.

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose requiring a government permit before any march. How do you respond?
choices: Allow people to assemble and speak without needing a permit first.
Require brief, cost-free notice only for large gatherings.
Require a permit that officials are free to withhold.
Allow only marches that officials have approved.

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose requiring newspapers to get a license before they may publish. How do you respond?
choices: Let anyone publish without a license
Require a simple registration to publish
Require a license that officials may deny
Permit only papers the officials approve

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose requiring everyone to recite an official message. How do you respond?
choices: Let people speak or stay silent as they choose
Request the message, but allow people to opt out
Require most people to recite it
Penalize anyone who refuses

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose banning any criticism of town officials. How do you respond?
choices: Let people criticize officials freely
Allow it, but punish disrespectful speech
Ban harsh criticism of officials
Punish anyone who speaks against officials

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some want to require official approval before people can hold a rally. What do you do?
choices: Protect the right to gather and speak
Allow rallies, but require a permit each time
Approve only the rallies the town agrees with
Ban public gatherings that criticize leaders

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose banning shops from advertising their prices and deals. How do you respond?
choices: Let shops inform people about their goods
Allow ads, but restrict them heavily
Ban most advertising
Permit only officially approved ads

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose letting the town decide which charities may ask for donations. How do you respond?
choices: Let any honest charity seek support
Allow it, but impose heavy registration
Permit only approved charities to collect
Ban the charities the town disfavors

10.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
10.body: Some propose requiring officials to approve online posts before they are shared. How do you respond?
choices: Let people post without prior approval
Allow posts, but pre-screen some of them
Require approval before posting
Permit only approved messages online

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Rising costs strain households. Proposals range from competition to subsidies, rationing, and single-payer. What is your approach?
choices: Enable competition among providers and require clear, upfront prices.
Keep the current system, but expand subsidies.
Tighten regulation and limit which treatments are available.
Set up single-payer government control of care and prices.

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Qualified nurses could handle more routine care, but current rules reserve those tasks for physicians. What is your decision?
choices: Broaden the range of tasks that qualified nurses are allowed to perform.
Relax the rules for just a handful of tasks.
Keep the strict rules on who is allowed to treat patients.
Have the government assign all medical tasks from the center.

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Medicine is much cheaper in a neighboring market, and some want to ban buying it there. How do you respond?
choices: Let residents buy safe, lower-cost medicine from other markets.
Allow it, but only under strict restrictions.
Ban imports of the cheaper medicine.
Grant the government a monopoly on medicine.

10.title: Health Care
10.body: A certificate-of-need rule caps new clinics, and a nurse wants to open one. How do you respond?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete.
Permit only a limited number of new clinics.
Keep the cap on new clinics.
Put the operation of all clinics in government hands.

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Medicine is expensive, and some propose legal price controls. How do you respond?
choices: Let more sellers compete, which brings prices down
Cap the prices of a few medicines
Set strict price controls on medicine
Have the government produce all medicine

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Some propose forcing everyone to buy the same single health plan. How do you respond?
choices: Let people choose the plan that fits them
Require a basic plan, but allow other choices
Force everyone into an identical plan
Have the government provide the only plan

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Some propose making one government office the only provider of care. How do you respond?
choices: Let many clinics and doctors compete
Allow private care alongside a large public office
Route most care through one office
Make the government the only provider

10.title: Health Care
10.body: People want affordable, quality care, and some want one public system to run it all. What do you do?
choices: Let clinics and doctors compete, with real choice for patients
Keep private care alongside a large public option
Route most care through one public system
Make the government the only provider

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Some propose banning private clinics so that all care is public. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public care exist side by side
Allow private care, but under heavy rules
Discourage private clinics
Ban private clinics

10.title: Health Care
10.body: Some propose forcing doctors to accept only government-set fees. How do you respond?
choices: Let doctors and patients agree on fees
Set fees, but allow some exceptions
Impose one fee schedule on all doctors
Have the government employ all doctors

10.title: Health Care
10.body: A charity clinic offers low-cost care, and the board moves to shut it down. How do you respond?
choices: Let the clinic keep serving patients
Allow it, but under heavy rules
Restrict the clinic in order to protect hospitals
Shut down the charity clinic

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Domestic producers want protective tariffs, which are taxes on imports, while consumers and exporters warn about higher prices and retaliation. How do you weigh these interests?
choices: Keep trade open so comparative advantage decides where goods are produced
Allow trade but add a moderate tariff, a tax on imports
Impose heavy tariffs to shield established domestic producers
Put all trade under government ownership and central control

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: A trader buys surplus goods cheaply in a distant market and resells them locally at a markup. Critics call it profiteering. How do you respond?
choices: Permit it, because this arbitrage moves goods toward their highest-valued uses and rewards the trader for bearing risk
Permit it but cap the markup by law
Ban resale above the original purchase price
Place all exchange under centralized government direction

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: A neighboring region exports low-cost toys, and domestic manufacturers lobby for protection. How do you respond?
choices: Allow the imports so consumers gain lower prices and greater variety
Impose a moderate tariff on the imports
Impose a prohibitive tariff to keep the goods out
Ban the import of foreign-made goods

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Your region produces bread efficiently and a neighbor produces cloth efficiently, yet some champion complete self-sufficiency. How do you respond?
choices: Specialize and trade, since comparative advantage makes both regions richer
Trade only a little while producing most goods at home
Give up trade and produce everything locally
Put all trade under central planning

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Officials propose an import quota that caps how many foreign goods can enter the market. How do you respond?
choices: Allow imports freely so consumers get lower prices
Set a high quota that does not yet restrict supply
Impose a tight quota that limits imports
Ban all goods from rival countries

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Officials propose requiring that public funds be spent only with local vendors. How do you respond?
choices: Buy from whichever vendor offers the best value
Give local vendors a small preference
Require local sourcing in most cases
Ban buying from any outside vendor

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Other regions want to buy our grain, but some demand it stay at home. How do you respond?
choices: Let producers sell to any willing buyer
Allow exports up to a set volume cap
Keep most grain from being exported
Ban grain exports outright

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Import competition threatens an old industry that lobbies for protection, while consumers pay higher prices. How do you decide?
choices: Keep trade open and let comparative advantage decide where goods are produced
Grant a temporary tariff, a tax on imports, that phases out over time to ease adjustment
Impose lasting protective tariffs
Nationalize the industry to keep it alive

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: A neighboring region put tariffs on our goods, and some urge heavy retaliation. How do you respond?
choices: Keep our trade open regardless of their tariffs
Impose a small matching tariff
Retaliate with heavy tariffs
Cut off all trade with that region

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: Some propose placing all foreign trade in the government's hands alone. How do you respond?
choices: Let individuals and businesses trade freely
Allow private trade alongside a public trader
Let the government handle most trade
Make the government the sole trader

11.title: Free Markets & Trade
11.body: A neighboring region sells goods below cost, and domestic sellers demand they be shut out. How do you respond?
choices: Let consumers enjoy the lower prices
Shut them out in select cases
Block the cheap goods to protect domestic sellers
Ban all imports from that region

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: A budget surplus tempts lawmakers to create new entitlements, while others urge restraint, and each program locks in recurring costs. Your posture?
choices: Keep taxes low and broad-based, and fund only essential functions
Raise revenue modestly to fund targeted programs
Expand both spending and taxation to pay for broad entitlements
Centralize government control over the economy's resources

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Lawmakers push a steeply progressive tax on top earners, while advisers warn that mobile capital and talent may leave. What is your approach?
choices: Keep tax rates low and broad-based to preserve the investment and human capital that fund prosperity
Add a modest surcharge on the highest incomes
Enact a steep tax narrowly targeted at top earners
Give officials discretion over each taxpayer's bill

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Backers want a public subsidy for a stadium whose payoff to taxpayers is doubtful. How do you proceed?
choices: Insist that private investors finance and build it
Split the cost between taxpayers and the owners
Cover most of the cost from public funds
Fund the entire project with tax revenue

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Backers want fare-free transit paid for by a new tax. How do you proceed?
choices: Charge modest fares so riders bear the cost of what they use
Combine modest fares with a partial subsidy
Provide fare-free service funded by higher taxes
Hand all transit over to public operation

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: A major employer asks for a targeted subsidy, a government cash handout, to stay in town. How do you respond?
choices: Say no and keep the same tax rules for every firm
Offer a small, temporary subsidy
Grant a large subsidy
Have the government take over the company

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Some want steeply progressive tax rates on high earners. How do you respond?
choices: Keep a low tax rate applied broadly to everyone
Add a small surcharge on top earners
Impose steeply progressive rates at the top
Let officials set each person's rate at their discretion

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Some propose taxing most residents to hand out an equal cash transfer to everyone. How do you respond?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn
Provide a small transfer paid for by taxes
Tax heavily to fund large transfers
Let the state distribute all income

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Advocates propose funding expansive transfers through steeply progressive taxation. How do you respond?
choices: Keep low, broad-based taxes that fund core public goods
Introduce a modest transfer funded by a narrow tax
Fund large transfers with heavy progressive taxation
Let the state allocate the nation's income

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: Some propose a steep excise tax, a so-called sin tax, on a disfavored product. How do you respond?
choices: Inform people and respect the choices they make
Put a small tax on it
Impose a steep tax on it
Ban the product

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: A new transfer program pays so generously that some people stop working. How do you respond?
choices: Support people while keeping the reward for working
Pay generously with almost no work requirements
Pay so generously that working no longer pays
Pay everyone with no conditions

11.title: Taxes & Spending
11.body: The town proposes a large new program funded by heavy borrowing. How do you respond?
choices: Keep spending within current means
Borrow modestly to fund it
Borrow heavily for the program
Borrow without any limit

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Persistent underperformance in monopoly public schools has sparked debate over funding, competition, and central control. Your policy?
choices: Make funding portable so families can choose among schools
Expand charter and specialized school options
Send more funding to the existing school monopoly
Impose one uniform curriculum under central control

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Families want room to homeschool or form learning co-ops, while officials debate what limits to allow. Where do you stand?
choices: Affirm educational pluralism so families can choose the model that best fits their children
Permit it but require detailed reporting
Steer families back toward the assigned district school
Require every child to attend the district school

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Reformers propose portable vouchers that let funding follow students to the schools they choose. What is your position?
choices: Create portable vouchers so funding follows each child
Allow vouchers but attach extensive conditions
Keep assigning students to schools by where they live
Limit attendance to the assigned district school

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Reformers propose one mandated curriculum for every school. What is your position?
choices: Let schools and parents choose what works for them
Set minimal standards while preserving flexibility
Standardize most instruction across all schools
Force one identical curriculum on every school

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Families want to open a charter school that follows a distinct teaching model. How do you respond?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Limit charters to shield district schools from competition
Allow only the assigned district school

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Some propose one mandatory standardized test for every school. How do you respond?
choices: Let schools and families measure progress in flexible ways
Adopt one light, shared test
Require intensive testing in every school
Base every decision on one single exam

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Some propose banning private schools so all students attend one single system. How do you respond?
choices: Let private schools operate alongside public ones
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Discourage private schooling
Ban private schools

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Reformers propose funding that follows the student, and the bureaucracy resists losing control of enrollment. How do you proceed?
choices: Attach funding to the student and allow broad choice
Pilot limited choice within tight caps
Keep assigning students by where they live
Require one uniform state curriculum for all schools

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Families want education savings accounts for tutoring, materials, and courses. How do you respond?
choices: Let funds follow the child to whatever needs the family chooses
Allow them, but under strict limits
Confine the funding to one single system
Have officials direct every dollar

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: Officials propose nationalizing a private college. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public institutions coexist
Nationalize it but leave it some autonomy
Run the college through the state
Ban private colleges

11.title: Education & School Choice
11.body: A capable tutor without a state credential wants to teach. How do you respond?
choices: Let parents choose the tutors they trust
Allow it but require paperwork
Require a costly credential to tutor
Allow only state-credentialed teachers

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Sustained inflation erodes real wages and savings, and advisers split among monetary restraint, price controls, and further expansion. Your response?
choices: Restrain the growth of the money supply to anchor prices
Slow the monetary expansion gradually
Impose legal price controls
Expand the money supply across the board

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Officials propose paying for a civic festival by printing new currency. How do you respond?
choices: Fund it from real revenue, or do not hold it at all
Approve a small, one-time issuance of currency
Print whatever amount the festival requires
Make routine money-printing the standard way to fund public events

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Facing a bread shortage, officials propose a binding price ceiling. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise to clear the market so suppliers restock the shelves
Impose a moderate price ceiling
Set a strict ceiling below the market price
Nationalize distribution and ration the supply

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Amid stubborn inflation, officials propose a year-long freeze on all prices. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices adjust and rein in the growth of the money supply
Freeze a limited set of prices for a short time
Impose a full year-long freeze on all prices
Set all prices centrally

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Officials propose debasing the currency by mixing in cheap metal to expand the money supply. How do you respond?
choices: Keep a sound, full-value currency
Allow a small amount of debasement
Permit heavy debasement
Let officials alter the currency whenever they choose

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Politicians want direct control over monetary policy to help them win elections. How do you respond?
choices: Keep stable monetary rules that are insulated from politics
Allow limited political influence over policy
Let officials set policy to win votes
Allow spending financed by printing money at will

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Some propose a universal cash payment funded by expanding the money supply. How do you respond?
choices: Fund payments only from real revenue
Print modest sums for limited payments
Print large sums for substantial payments
Fund permanent payments through the printing press

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Persistent inflation erodes real wages. Some urge monetary restraint while others want further expansion. How do you respond?
choices: Anchor to stable, rule-bound growth in the money supply
Allow modest additional expansion
Expand the money supply aggressively to fund spending
Impose broad price controls

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Officials propose abolishing cash and requiring a single digital currency. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use cash or apps as they prefer
Promote digital payment while preserving some cash
Require a single official digital currency
Watch and control every transaction

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Officials propose forcing interest rates far below market to expand cheap credit. How do you respond?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and how people value money now versus later
Nudge rates modestly lower
Force rates far below the market level
Set all rates by decree

11.title: Money & Inflation
11.body: Officials propose fixing the currency's value by decree. How do you respond?
choices: Let the currency's value float freely
Guide the value modestly
Fix the value by decree
Punish trades made at any other rate

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: A would-be entrepreneur faces costly, year-long licensing while established firms defend the barriers. How do you balance safety against entry?
choices: Streamline licensing while keeping the standards that address real safety
Keep the licensing system but grant a few selective exemptions
Intensify inspections and compliance requirements
Create a government-run enterprise instead

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: A prospective food-cart operator faces a costly, drawn-out licensing process, and reformers question why it exists. How do you proceed?
choices: Narrow licensing to genuine safety concerns, which lowers the cost of entry
Keep the license but grant discretionary waivers to a select few
Add still more fees and required coursework
Grant a single city-run monopoly over street vending

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: Ride-share platforms compete with licensed taxis, whose operators demand a ban. How do you proceed?
choices: Let both compete under the same neutral rules
Allow the platforms but under heavy regulation
Cap how many platforms are allowed
Ban ride-share platforms entirely

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: Hair braiders must complete a costly, year-long licensing program. How do you proceed?
choices: Repeal the requirement so braiders can work freely
Keep a short, inexpensive safety course
Keep the long, costly license
License only a limited number of braiders

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: A large chain wants to open, and existing shops demand it be kept out. How do you respond?
choices: Allow it to enter and compete under neutral rules
Admit it, but under heavy regulation
Block the chain to protect existing shops
Give officials discretion to decide who may enter

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: Moving companies must get a burdensome, costly license before they can operate. How do you respond?
choices: Remove the barrier so movers can start working
Keep a brief, low-cost safety check
Keep the burdensome, costly license
License only a fixed number of movers

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: Residents must get a costly permit before they can run a home business. How do you respond?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely
Require brief, low-cost registration
Keep the costly permit
Ban home-based businesses

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: Compliance costs and licensing deter new entrants while established firms lobby to keep the barriers. How do you respond?
choices: Lower barriers to entry and streamline licensing
Trim a few requirements while keeping most
Keep the existing tangle of regulation
Require case-by-case state approval for every firm

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: After a disaster, sellers raise water prices, and some propose anti-gouging laws. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise so more supply rushes in
Allow only small increases
Ban any price increase
Nationalize and ration all water

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: A firm grew large because customers liked it, and some demand it be broken up. How do you respond?
choices: Let it keep competing, as long as it plays fair
Impose extra rules on it just for being big
Break up the firm simply for being large
Put the firm under state operation

11.title: Business & Regulation
11.body: Opening even a modest shop takes months of forms and permits. How do you respond?
