choices: Cut the red tape so shops can open readily
Keep a brief, simple registration
Keep the drawn-out paperwork
Let officials approve each shop one by one

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Advocates demand a steep minimum wage, while economists warn it could cost low-skill workers their jobs. Your choice?
choices: Pursue economic growth so employers compete and bid up wages
Raise the minimum wage modestly and watch what happens to employment
Mandate a steep wage floor right away
Have the government set all wages

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Claiming to protect full-time jobs, some propose curbing part-time and gig work. What is your decision?
choices: Preserve flexible work and freedom of contract
Allow flexible work only under strict constraints
Sharply restrict gig and part-time work
Allow only approved full-time positions

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Advocates urge a large increase in the statutory minimum wage. How do you respond?
choices: Let wages rise with productivity and competition among employers for workers
Raise the statutory minimum wage moderately
Mandate a steep increase in the minimum wage
Give the government authority over all wages

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Advocates propose a universal government job guarantee. How do you respond?
choices: Support private enterprise so it can expand and hire
Add some public jobs alongside private ones
Make most employment public
Put all employment under state direction

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Some propose requiring every worker to join a union. How do you respond?
choices: Protect each worker's freedom to join a union or not
Allow unions but pressure workers to sign up
Force most workers to become members
Give the union monopoly control over who gets hired

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Some want to require extensive paid leave from every employer. How do you respond?
choices: Let workers and employers negotiate benefits themselves
Require a modest minimum of paid leave
Require a generous paid-leave benefit
Give officials authority over all benefits

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Some propose a legal ceiling on how much a person may earn. How do you respond?
choices: Let people earn as much as they legitimately can
Allow high earnings but tax the top punitively
Cap how much anyone may earn
Give officials authority over all pay

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: A proposal would set a high uniform wage floor to raise incomes. How do you respond?
choices: Let wages emerge from voluntary agreement
Set a modest wage floor, adjusted cautiously over time
Impose a high uniform wage floor
Set all pay centrally

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Some propose requiring employers to hire by fixed quotas. How do you respond?
choices: Let employers hire the best fit for each role
Set soft hiring targets
Impose rigid hiring quotas
Have officials assign all workers to jobs

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: Some propose a law barring employers from making layoffs. How do you respond?
choices: Let firms adjust their staffing as conditions change
Allow layoffs but impose heavy penalties
Ban nearly all layoffs
Put all hiring and firing under state control

11.title: Jobs & Wages
11.body: New machinery could do certain jobs, and some demand a ban to protect employment. How do you respond?
choices: Allow the machines, and let workers move into new roles
Allow the machinery but slow its adoption
Ban the machinery to protect existing jobs
Put all machinery under state control

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: An upstream plant's discharge imposes costs on downstream residents, a classic externality. Remedies range from liability to nationalization. The principled one?
choices: Enforce property rights so the polluter bears the cost of the harm
Issue detailed command-and-control regulations
Transfer the plant to public operation
Seize the plant and the surrounding land

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: The city proposes using eminent domain to take homes and hand them to a private developer. How do you decide?
choices: Protect owners and limit development to land sold voluntarily
Take the property but pay compensation
Justify the taking under a broad public-benefit rationale
Let the city seize property at its own unchecked discretion

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: A factory's waste contaminates a shared river. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Hold the factory liable for the harm it imposes on others
Ask the factory to cut pollution voluntarily
Allow unrestricted discharge
Place the factory under city ownership

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: A commonly owned lake is being drained by overfishing. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Assign clear, well-defined fishing rights to prevent depletion
Ask fishers to voluntarily reduce their catch
Allow anyone to fish without limit
Take the lake into public ownership

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: Rents are climbing fast, and some propose rent control. How do you respond?
choices: Make it easier to build new housing to relieve rents
Impose mild rent control
Impose strict rent control
Put all housing under government ownership

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: Zoning restrictions stop owners from building new housing on their own land. How do you respond?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their property
Allow building under limited restrictions
Keep strict limits on construction
Give officials control over all construction

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: Some land sits idle, and officials propose seizing it. How do you respond?
choices: Leave it with the owner to hold, sell, or develop
Tax the idle land
Seize the idle land
Transfer title to all idle land to the state

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: Officials propose seizing underused parcels of land to direct how they are used. How do you respond?
choices: Keep land in private hands and let owners decide its use
Levy punitive taxes on idle holdings
Restrict permitted uses by decree
Seize the parcels and manage them publicly

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: Some propose forcing landowners to allow public passage across their property. How do you respond?
choices: Respect owners and invite them to share voluntarily
Force access across a narrow strip
Force public access to most land
Let anyone use any land at any time

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: Factories pollute. Some favor a blanket ban, others a market in permits. How do you respond?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup happens where it costs the least
Apply one uniform mandate to every factory
Allow unrestricted pollution
Put every factory under state operation

11.title: Property & Environment
11.body: A shared aquifer is being drained by unrestricted pumping. How do you respond?
choices: Assign clear water rights to prevent the aquifer from being drained
Ask users to cut back voluntarily
Allow unrestricted pumping
Seize the aquifer into public control

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Households want safe savings and reliable credit, while policymakers debate competition, guarantees, and state banking. Your framework?
choices: Foster competitive private banking under clear, transparent rules
Allow private banking but have the government guarantee all risky lending
Establish a single state-owned monopoly bank
Centralize all monetary and credit authority in the state

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Officials propose legal caps on lending rates, while lenders warn this will ration credit. What framework do you adopt?
choices: Let interest rates reflect risk so credit stays available across the full range of borrowers
Cap rates modestly on a few select loan types
Set strict caps well below market rates
Give the government power to set all interest rates

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: A bank left insolvent by speculation asks for a public bailout. How do you respond?
choices: Let it fail while protecting insured depositors
Offer a limited emergency loan facility
Grant a full bailout
Put the banking sector under state control

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Officials propose a new tax on the interest earned from savings. How do you respond?
choices: Leave savings untaxed so households can build a financial cushion
Tax savings interest modestly
Tax savings interest heavily
Place custody of all savings with the government

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Officials propose requiring banks to issue a set quota of risky loans. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend based on real risk
Require a small quota of such loans
Impose large lending quotas
Put all lending under government direction

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Some propose placing all banking in a single state-run institution. How do you respond?
choices: Let many banks compete for customers
Allow several banks alongside a public one
Let the state bank dominate the market
Make the state bank the only institution

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Banks charge fees, and some propose a low legal ceiling on them. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks compete to bring fees down
Cap a select set of fees
Impose strict fee ceilings
Give officials authority over all fees

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Policymakers debate whether to guarantee all deposits, cap fees, or expand a state bank. What framework do you set?
choices: Enable competition under simple, transparent rules
Guarantee all deposits without limit
Impose binding ceilings on fees and rates
Consolidate banking under a state monopoly

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Some propose a tax on money held in savings to push people to spend. How do you respond?
choices: Let people save freely without penalty
Put a small charge on large balances
Charge a tax on most savings
Confiscate a portion of idle savings

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Some propose requiring banks to lend only within the town. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend wherever the money is put to best use
Give local lending a modest preference
Require nearly all lending to stay local
Put all lending under official direction

11.title: Banking & Saving
11.body: Officials propose capping how much depositors may withdraw. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use their own money freely
Restrict only the largest withdrawals
Cap withdrawals across the board
Freeze deposit accounts

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Critical news coverage prompts officials to seek restrictions, pitting the government's reputation against the public's right to information. Your response?
choices: Protect free expression and a free press, including dissent
Allow publication but subject it to prior restraint, meaning pre-publication approval
Ban coverage that discredits officials
Place the press under government control

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: A polarizing speaker asks to use the public square, and a group demands a ban. How do you respond?
choices: Protect the speech and reject any ban based on its viewpoint
Allow it only after officials approve the subject matter in advance
Ban speakers the majority finds objectionable
Allow only officially approved speakers

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: A group petitions to remove a book from the library's collection. How do you respond?
choices: Keep the book and leave the reading choice to individuals
Keep it but make it harder to access
Remove titles a vocal group finds objectionable
Limit the collection to officially approved works

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose requiring a government permit before any march. How do you respond?
choices: Allow assembly and speech without prior government authorization
Require brief, free notice for large gatherings
Require a permit officials can deny at their discretion
Allow only officially approved marches

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose requiring newspapers to get a license before they may publish. How do you respond?
choices: Allow publication with no license at all
Require only simple registration to publish
Require a license that officials may deny
Allow only officially approved outlets

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose forcing everyone to recite an official affirmation. How do you respond?
choices: Let people speak or stay silent as they choose
Ask for the affirmation but allow opt-outs
Require most people to recite it
Punish those who refuse

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose banning criticism of public officials. How do you respond?
choices: Let people criticize officials freely
Allow it but punish disrespectful expression
Ban pointed criticism of officials
Punish anyone who speaks against officials

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: A measure would criminalize any expression officials deem disrespectful of the state. How do you respond?
choices: Protect expression, including sharp dissent
Allow speech but punish disrespectful forms of it
Ban pointed criticism of the state
Criminalize dissent against officials

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose banning merchants from advertising their prices and offers. How do you respond?
choices: Let merchants tell people about their goods
Allow advertising but restrict it heavily
Ban most advertising
Allow only officially approved ads

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose that officials license which charities may ask for donations. How do you respond?
choices: Let any honest charity seek support
Allow it but impose heavy registration
Allow only approved charities to collect
Ban charities the state disfavors

11.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
11.body: Some propose requiring official pre-approval of online posts. How do you respond?
choices: Let people post without prior approval
Allow posts but pre-screen some of them
Require approval before anyone can post
Allow only approved messages online

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Rising health costs strain households, and proposals span competition, subsidies, rationing, and single-payer control. Your approach?
choices: Enable competition among providers and require price transparency
Keep the current system but expand subsidies
Tighten regulation and restrict which treatments are available
Establish single-payer government control of both care and pricing

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Credentialed nurses could handle more routine care, but scope-of-practice rules reserve those services for physicians. What is your decision?
choices: Expand the scope of practice for credentialed nurses
Loosen the rules for a limited set of tasks
Keep the restrictive scope-of-practice rules
Centrally assign all clinical responsibilities

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Medicines are noticeably cheaper in a neighboring region, and some want to ban buying them across the border. How do you respond?
choices: Let residents buy safe, lower-cost medicine abroad
Allow it but under heavy restrictions
Ban imports of the cheaper medicine
Create a government monopoly over medicine

11.title: Health Care
11.body: A certificate-of-need rule caps new clinics, and a nurse wants to open one. How do you respond?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete
Approve only a limited number of new clinics
Keep the cap on new clinics
Put all clinics under government operation

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Medicine is expensive, and some propose legal price controls. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage competition among sellers to bring prices down
Cap the prices of certain medicines
Impose strict price controls
Put all medicine production under the government

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Some propose requiring everyone to buy one single health plan. How do you respond?
choices: Let people choose the plan that suits them
Require a basic plan while still preserving some choice
Force everyone into one identical plan
Provide coverage through one government plan only

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Some propose making one government office the sole provider of care. How do you respond?
choices: Let many clinics and physicians compete
Allow private care alongside a large public office
Route most care through a single office
Make government the sole provider

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Reformers propose consolidating care under a single public authority to control cost. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage competition among providers, with patients free to choose
Allow private care alongside a dominant public plan
Route all care through a single authority
Make the state the sole provider

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Some propose banning private clinics so that all care is public. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public care coexist
Allow private care under heavy regulation
Discourage private clinics
Ban private clinics

11.title: Health Care
11.body: Some propose forcing physicians to accept only government-set fees. How do you respond?
choices: Let physicians and patients agree on fees themselves
Set fees but allow some exceptions
Impose one fee schedule on all physicians
Have the state employ all physicians

11.title: Health Care
11.body: A charity clinic provides low-cost care, and the board moves to close it. How do you respond?
choices: Let the clinic keep serving patients
Allow it, but under heavy regulation
Restrict the clinic to protect hospitals
Close the charity clinic

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Domestic producers facing competition from a neighboring region want protective tariffs, while consumers and exporters warn of retaliation and higher prices. How do you weigh these interests?
choices: Keep trade open and let comparative advantage direct each region to produce what it makes most efficiently.
Allow trade but add a modest tariff, a small tax on imports, to cushion local industry.
Impose steep tariffs to shield established domestic producers from foreign competition.
Nationalize trade and have the state centrally administer every cross-border exchange.

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: An arbitrageur buys goods cheaply in one market and resells them in yours at a markup. Critics call the practice exploitative. How do you respond?
choices: Permit it, since arbitrage moves goods toward their highest-valued uses.
Permit it but impose a ceiling on permissible markup.
Prohibit resale above the original purchase price.
Have the state monopolize purchasing and distribution.

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: A neighboring jurisdiction exports inexpensive toys, and domestic manufacturers lobby for a protective tariff. How do you respond?
choices: Permit the imports so consumers capture lower prices and wider choice.
Levy a modest tariff on the imports.
Impose a prohibitive tariff to exclude them.
Ban goods originating outside the jurisdiction.

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Your jurisdiction produces bread efficiently and a neighbor produces cloth efficiently, yet some advocate complete self-sufficiency. How do you respond?
choices: Specialize and trade, since comparative advantage enriches both parties.
Trade marginally while producing most goods domestically.
Forgo trade and produce everything locally.
Subject all trade to central planning.

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Officials propose an import quota that caps how many foreign goods can enter the market. How do you respond?
choices: Allow imports freely so consumers get lower prices
Set a high quota that does not yet restrict supply
Impose a tight quota that limits imports
Ban all goods from rival countries

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Officials propose requiring that public funds be spent only with local vendors. How do you respond?
choices: Buy from whichever vendor offers the best value
Give local vendors a small preference
Require local sourcing in most cases
Ban buying from any outside vendor

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Other regions want to buy our grain, but some demand it stay at home. How do you respond?
choices: Let producers sell to any willing buyer
Allow exports up to a set volume cap
Keep most grain from being exported
Ban grain exports outright

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Import competition threatens an old industry that lobbies for protection, while consumers pay higher prices. How do you decide?
choices: Keep trade open and let comparative advantage decide where goods are produced
Grant a temporary tariff, a tax on imports, that phases out over time to ease adjustment
Impose lasting protective tariffs
Nationalize the industry to keep it alive

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: A neighboring region put tariffs on our goods, and some urge heavy retaliation. How do you respond?
choices: Keep our trade open regardless of their tariffs
Impose a small matching tariff
Retaliate with heavy tariffs
Cut off all trade with that region

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: Some propose placing all foreign trade in the government's hands alone. How do you respond?
choices: Let individuals and businesses trade freely
Allow private trade alongside a public trader
Let the government handle most trade
Make the government the sole trader

12.title: Free Markets & Trade
12.body: A neighboring region sells goods below cost, and domestic sellers demand they be shut out. How do you respond?
choices: Let consumers enjoy the lower prices
Shut them out in select cases
Block the cheap goods to protect domestic sellers
Ban all imports from that region

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: A budget surplus tempts legislators toward costly new entitlements, while fiscal conservatives urge restraint because each program carries recurring obligations. What posture do you adopt?
choices: Hold the line on low, broad-based taxes and fund only essential government functions.
Raise revenue modestly to finance a few targeted programs.
Substantially expand spending and taxation to fund broad new entitlements.
Centralize fiscal authority and have the state direct most economic resources.

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Legislators propose a steeply progressive wealth tax targeting the highest earners. Advisers warn of capital flight. What is your fiscal judgment?
choices: Maintain a low, broad-based tax to retain capital and talent.
Impose a modest surcharge on top earners.
Levy a steep, targeted wealth tax.
Vest officials with discretion over each taxpayer's burden.

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Boosters seek public financing for a new stadium, while analysts question the return to taxpayers. How do you proceed?
choices: Require private capital to finance and build it.
Apportion the cost between taxpayers and owners.
Fund the majority of the cost publicly.
Underwrite the entire project with tax revenue.

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Proponents seek fare-free public buses financed by a new tax. How do you proceed?
choices: Levy modest fares so riders bear the cost.
Blend modest fares with a partial subsidy.
Provide fare-free service through higher taxes.
Place all transportation under public operation.

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: A major employer asks for a targeted subsidy, a government cash handout, to stay in town. How do you respond?
choices: Say no and keep the same tax rules for every firm
Offer a small, temporary subsidy
Grant a large subsidy
Have the government take over the company

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Some want steeply progressive tax rates on high earners. How do you respond?
choices: Keep a low tax rate applied broadly to everyone
Add a small surcharge on top earners
Impose steeply progressive rates at the top
Let officials set each person's rate at their discretion

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Some propose taxing most residents to hand out an equal cash transfer to everyone. How do you respond?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn
Provide a small transfer paid for by taxes
Tax heavily to fund large transfers
Let the state distribute all income

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Advocates propose funding expansive transfers through steeply progressive taxation. How do you respond?
choices: Keep low, broad-based taxes that fund core public goods
Introduce a modest transfer funded by a narrow tax
Fund large transfers with heavy progressive taxation
Let the state allocate the nation's income

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: Some propose a steep excise tax, a so-called sin tax, on a disfavored product. How do you respond?
choices: Inform people and respect the choices they make
Put a small tax on it
Impose a steep tax on it
Ban the product

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: A new transfer program pays so generously that some people stop working. How do you respond?
choices: Support people while keeping the reward for working
Pay generously with almost no work requirements
Pay so generously that working no longer pays
Pay everyone with no conditions

12.title: Taxes & Spending
12.body: The town proposes a large new program funded by heavy borrowing. How do you respond?
choices: Keep spending within current means
Borrow modestly to fund it
Borrow heavily for the program
Borrow without any limit

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Persistent underperformance in the district's monopoly schools prompts reform calls. Stakeholders dispute whether the remedy is funding, competition, or centralization. What policy do you pursue?
choices: Enact portable funding so families can choose among competing schools.
Expand chartered and specialized options within the existing public system.
Substantially increase funding to the existing school monopoly.
Impose a uniform national curriculum under centralized control.

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Families request the latitude to homeschool or form small learning cooperatives. Officials debate how much to permit. What is your stance?
choices: Affirm educational pluralism and let families choose their own model.
Allow it under burdensome reporting requirements.
Discourage alternatives and funnel students to the district.
Prohibit alternatives and compel uniform attendance.

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Reformers propose vouchers that let families direct funding to the school of their choice. What is your position?
choices: Enact portable vouchers so funding follows the child.
Permit vouchers subject to extensive conditions.
Retain assignment of students to district schools.
Permit attendance only at the assigned district school.

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Reformers propose a single mandated curriculum for every school. What is your position?
choices: Permit schools and parents to choose what works.
Set minimal standards while preserving flexibility.
Standardize most instruction across schools.
Impose one identical curriculum on all schools.

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Families want to open a charter school that follows a distinct teaching model. How do you respond?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Limit charters to shield district schools from competition
Allow only the assigned district school

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Some propose one mandatory standardized test for every school. How do you respond?
choices: Let schools and families measure progress in flexible ways
Adopt one light, shared test
Require intensive testing in every school
Base every decision on one single exam

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Some propose banning private schools so all students attend one single system. How do you respond?
choices: Let private schools operate alongside public ones
Allow them, but under heavy restrictions
Discourage private schooling
Ban private schools

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Reformers propose funding that follows the student, and the bureaucracy resists losing control of enrollment. How do you proceed?
choices: Attach funding to the student and allow broad choice
Pilot limited choice within tight caps
Keep assigning students by where they live
Require one uniform state curriculum for all schools

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Families want education savings accounts for tutoring, materials, and courses. How do you respond?
choices: Let funds follow the child to whatever needs the family chooses
Allow them, but under strict limits
Confine the funding to one single system
Have officials direct every dollar

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: Officials propose nationalizing a private college. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public institutions coexist
Nationalize it but leave it some autonomy
Run the college through the state
Ban private colleges

12.title: Education & School Choice
12.body: A capable tutor without a state credential wants to teach. How do you respond?
choices: Let parents choose the tutors they trust
Allow it but require paperwork
Require a costly credential to tutor
Allow only state-credentialed teachers

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Sustained inflation is eroding real wages and savings. Advisers split between monetary restraint, price controls, and further expansion. What is your response?
choices: Restrain monetary growth to anchor prices and protect savers.
Decelerate monetary expansion gradually while monitoring prices.
Impose legal price controls across key goods.
Expand the money supply and distribute it broadly.

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Some officials propose monetary financing, printing money, to fund a civic festival. What is your response?
choices: Fund it from real revenue or forgo it.
Authorize a small, one-time printing of money.
Print whatever the festival requires.
Rely on routine money creation to fund expenditures.

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Amid a bread shortage, officials propose a binding price ceiling. How do you respond?
choices: Allow prices to clear so suppliers replenish stocks.
Impose a modest ceiling.
Set a stringent ceiling far below the market price.
Nationalize distribution and ration the supply.

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Amid persistent inflation, officials propose a year-long freeze on all prices. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices adjust and rein in monetary growth.
Freeze a narrow set of prices briefly.
Impose a comprehensive year-long freeze.
Administer every price centrally.

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Officials propose debasing the currency by mixing in cheap metal to expand the money supply. How do you respond?
choices: Keep a sound, full-value currency
Allow a small amount of debasement
Permit heavy debasement
Let officials alter the currency whenever they choose

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Politicians want direct control over monetary policy to help them win elections. How do you respond?
choices: Keep stable monetary rules that are insulated from politics
Allow limited political influence over policy
Let officials set policy to win votes
Allow spending financed by printing money at will

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Some propose a universal cash payment funded by expanding the money supply. How do you respond?
choices: Fund payments only from real revenue
Print modest sums for limited payments
Print large sums for substantial payments
Fund permanent payments through the printing press

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Persistent inflation erodes real wages. Some urge monetary restraint while others want further expansion. How do you respond?
choices: Anchor to stable, rule-bound growth in the money supply
Allow modest additional expansion
Expand the money supply aggressively to fund spending
Impose broad price controls

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Officials propose abolishing cash and requiring a single digital currency. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use cash or apps as they prefer
Promote digital payment while preserving some cash
Require a single official digital currency
Watch and control every transaction

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Officials propose forcing interest rates far below market to expand cheap credit. How do you respond?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and how people value money now versus later
Nudge rates modestly lower
Force rates far below the market level
Set all rates by decree

12.title: Money & Inflation
12.body: Officials propose fixing the currency's value by decree. How do you respond?
choices: Let the currency's value float freely
Guide the value modestly
Fix the value by decree
Punish trades made at any other rate

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: A would-be entrepreneur confronts a costly, year-long licensing process. Incumbents defend the barriers as quality assurance. How do you reconcile safety with entry?
choices: Streamline licensing to remove barriers while retaining genuine safety standards.
Keep the licensing regime but offer selective exemptions to a few applicants.
Intensify inspections and compliance requirements.
Establish a government-operated enterprise to meet demand.

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Operating a food cart requires an expensive, months-long occupational license. Reformers question its necessity. How do you proceed?
choices: Trim licensing to genuine safety rules, which lowers entry costs.
Retain licensing but waive it for select applicants.
Add more fees and mandatory coursework.
Establish a municipal monopoly on street food.

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Ride-share platforms now compete with licensed taxis, whose operators demand prohibition. How do you proceed?
choices: Allow both to compete under neutral rules.
Admit platforms under burdensome regulation.
Cap the number of platforms permitted to operate.
Prohibit ride-share platforms outright.

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Hair braiders must complete a costly, year-long licensing program. How do you proceed?
choices: Repeal the mandate so braiders may work freely.
Retain a brief, inexpensive safety course.
Preserve the lengthy, expensive license.
License only a limited number of braiders.

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: A large chain wants to open, and existing shops demand it be kept out. How do you respond?
choices: Allow it to enter and compete under neutral rules
Admit it, but under heavy regulation
Block the chain to protect existing shops
Give officials discretion to decide who may enter

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Moving companies must get a burdensome, costly license before they can operate. How do you respond?
choices: Remove the barrier so movers can start working
Keep a brief, low-cost safety check
Keep the burdensome, costly license
License only a fixed number of movers

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Residents must get a costly permit before they can run a home business. How do you respond?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely
Require brief, low-cost registration
Keep the costly permit
Ban home-based businesses

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Compliance costs and licensing deter new entrants while established firms lobby to keep the barriers. How do you respond?
choices: Lower barriers to entry and streamline licensing
Trim a few requirements while keeping most
Keep the existing tangle of regulation
Require case-by-case state approval for every firm

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: After a disaster, sellers raise water prices, and some propose anti-gouging laws. How do you respond?
choices: Let prices rise so more supply rushes in
Allow only small increases
Ban any price increase
Nationalize and ration all water

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: A firm grew large because customers liked it, and some demand it be broken up. How do you respond?
choices: Let it keep competing, as long as it plays fair
Impose extra rules on it just for being big
Break up the firm simply for being large
Put the firm under state operation

12.title: Business & Regulation
12.body: Opening even a modest shop takes months of forms and permits. How do you respond?
choices: Cut the red tape so shops can open readily
Keep a brief, simple registration
Keep the drawn-out paperwork
Let officials approve each shop one by one

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Advocates demand a steep legal minimum wage to address low pay, while economists warn it will cost low-skill workers their jobs. What do you choose?
choices: Pursue growth and competition so employers bid up wages.
Raise the minimum wage modestly and monitor employment.
Mandate a steep, immediate wage floor.
Have the state administer all compensation.

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: To protect full-time employment, some propose restricting part-time and gig work. What is your decision?
choices: Preserve labor-market flexibility and freedom of contract.
Permit flexible work under heavy restrictions.
Sharply curtail gig and part-time arrangements.
Permit only state-sanctioned full-time employment.

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Advocates press for a substantial increase in the legal minimum wage. How do you respond?
choices: Let wages rise with productivity and competition for labor.
Raise the legal minimum wage modestly.
Mandate a steep increase in the minimum wage.
Have the state administer all wages.

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Advocates propose a public guarantee of employment for everyone. How do you respond?
choices: Foster private enterprise so it can expand and hire.
Add a measure of public jobs alongside private ones.
Make most employment public.
Subject all employment to state direction.

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Some propose requiring every worker to join a union. How do you respond?
choices: Protect each worker's freedom to join a union or not
Allow unions but pressure workers to sign up
Force most workers to become members
Give the union monopoly control over who gets hired

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Some want to require extensive paid leave from every employer. How do you respond?
choices: Let workers and employers negotiate benefits themselves
Require a modest minimum of paid leave
Require a generous paid-leave benefit
Give officials authority over all benefits

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Some propose a legal ceiling on how much a person may earn. How do you respond?
choices: Let people earn as much as they legitimately can
Allow high earnings but tax the top punitively
Cap how much anyone may earn
Give officials authority over all pay

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: A proposal would set a high uniform wage floor to raise incomes. How do you respond?
choices: Let wages emerge from voluntary agreement
Set a modest wage floor, adjusted cautiously over time
Impose a high uniform wage floor
Set all pay centrally

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Some propose requiring employers to hire by fixed quotas. How do you respond?
choices: Let employers hire the best fit for each role
Set soft hiring targets
Impose rigid hiring quotas
Have officials assign all workers to jobs

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: Some propose a law barring employers from making layoffs. How do you respond?
choices: Let firms adjust their staffing as conditions change
Allow layoffs but impose heavy penalties
Ban nearly all layoffs
Put all hiring and firing under state control

12.title: Jobs & Wages
12.body: New machinery could do certain jobs, and some demand a ban to protect employment. How do you respond?
choices: Allow the machines, and let workers move into new roles
Allow the machinery but slow its adoption
Ban the machinery to protect existing jobs
Put all machinery under state control

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: An upstream facility's discharge imposes costs on downstream residents, a classic externality. Proposals range from liability to nationalization. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Enforce property rights so the polluter bears the cost of the harm.
Issue detailed command-and-control regulations.
Transfer the facility to public operation.
Seize the facility and the surrounding land.

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: The municipality wants to use eminent domain to seize homes for a commercial development. How do you weigh the takings power?
choices: Protect owners and build only on land that is sold voluntarily.
Exercise takings but pay full compensation.
Invoke takings for the asserted public benefit.
Seize property at the state's discretion.

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: A factory's wastewater is contaminating a shared river. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Hold the factory liable for the damage it inflicts on others.
Ask the factory to reduce its pollution voluntarily.
Permit unrestricted discharge into the river.
Nationalize the factory under municipal control.

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: A commonly held lake is being overfished. What is the principled remedy?
choices: Assign defined fishing rights to prevent depletion.
Appeal for voluntary reductions in the catch.
Permit unlimited extraction by anyone.
Seize the lake into public ownership.

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: Rents are climbing fast, and some propose rent control. How do you respond?
choices: Make it easier to build new housing to relieve rents
Impose mild rent control
Impose strict rent control
Put all housing under government ownership

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: Zoning restrictions stop owners from building new housing on their own land. How do you respond?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their property
Allow building under limited restrictions
Keep strict limits on construction
Give officials control over all construction

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: Some land sits idle, and officials propose seizing it. How do you respond?
choices: Leave it with the owner to hold, sell, or develop
Tax the idle land
Seize the idle land
Transfer title to all idle land to the state

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: Officials propose seizing underused parcels of land to direct how they are used. How do you respond?
choices: Keep land in private hands and let owners decide its use
Levy punitive taxes on idle holdings
Restrict permitted uses by decree
Seize the parcels and manage them publicly

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: Some propose forcing landowners to allow public passage across their property. How do you respond?
choices: Respect owners and invite them to share voluntarily
Force access across a narrow strip
Force public access to most land
Let anyone use any land at any time

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: Factories pollute. Some favor a blanket ban, others a market in permits. How do you respond?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup happens where it costs the least
Apply one uniform mandate to every factory
Allow unrestricted pollution
Put every factory under state operation

12.title: Property & Environment
12.body: A shared aquifer is being drained by unrestricted pumping. How do you respond?
choices: Assign clear water rights to prevent the aquifer from being drained
Ask users to cut back voluntarily
Allow unrestricted pumping
Seize the aquifer into public control

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Households seek secure savings and credit. Policymakers debate competition, blanket guarantees, and state banking. What framework do you establish?
choices: Foster competitive, transparently regulated private banking.
Permit private banking but guarantee all risky lending.
Establish a monopoly state bank.
Centralize all monetary and credit authority.

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Officials propose usury ceilings that cap loan interest rates. Lenders warn this will ration credit. What framework do you set?
choices: Let rates reflect risk to keep credit broadly available.
Cap rates modestly on select loans.
Impose binding usury ceilings well below the market rate.
Have the state administer all interest rates.

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: A bank rendered insolvent by speculative bets seeks a public rescue. How do you respond?
choices: Allow it to fail while protecting insured depositors.
Provide a limited emergency loan.
Furnish a comprehensive bailout.
Place the entire banking sector under state control.

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Officials propose a new tax on interest earned from savings. How do you respond?
choices: Leave savings untaxed so households can accumulate a buffer.
Tax savings interest modestly.
Tax savings interest heavily.
Place custody of all savings with the state.

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Officials propose requiring banks to issue a set quota of risky loans. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend based on real risk
Require a small quota of such loans
Impose large lending quotas
Put all lending under government direction

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Some propose placing all banking in a single state-run institution. How do you respond?
choices: Let many banks compete for customers
Allow several banks alongside a public one
Let the state bank dominate the market
Make the state bank the only institution

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Banks charge fees, and some propose a low legal ceiling on them. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks compete to bring fees down
Cap a select set of fees
Impose strict fee ceilings
Give officials authority over all fees

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Policymakers debate whether to guarantee all deposits, cap fees, or expand a state bank. What framework do you set?
choices: Enable competition under simple, transparent rules
Guarantee all deposits without limit
Impose binding ceilings on fees and rates
Consolidate banking under a state monopoly

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Some propose a tax on money held in savings to push people to spend. How do you respond?
choices: Let people save freely without penalty
Put a small charge on large balances
Charge a tax on most savings
Confiscate a portion of idle savings

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Some propose requiring banks to lend only within the town. How do you respond?
choices: Let banks lend wherever the money is put to best use
Give local lending a modest preference
Require nearly all lending to stay local
Put all lending under official direction

12.title: Banking & Saving
12.body: Officials propose capping how much depositors may withdraw. How do you respond?
choices: Let people use their own money freely
Restrict only the largest withdrawals
Cap withdrawals across the board
Freeze deposit accounts

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Critical press coverage prompts officials to seek restrictions. Tension arises between the government's reputation and the public's right to information. How do you respond?
choices: Safeguard expression and a free press, including dissent.
Permit publication subject to prior review.
Prohibit coverage that discredits officials.
Place the press under state control.

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: A controversial speaker requests use of the public square, and some residents demand a ban. How do you respond?
choices: Protect the speech and reject viewpoint discrimination.
Permit it subject to topic pre-approval.
Ban speakers the majority opposes.
Permit only state-sanctioned speakers.

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: A faction petitions to remove a book from the public library's collection. How do you respond?
choices: Retain the book and leave the choice to read it to individuals.
Keep the book but restrict access to it.
Remove titles that a vocal segment finds objectionable.
Limit the collection to officially approved works only.

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose requiring a government permit before any march. How do you respond?
choices: Permit assembly and expression without prior permits.
Require brief, cost-free notice for large gatherings.
Require a permit that officials may withhold.
Sanction only officially approved marches.

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose requiring newspapers to get a license before they may publish. How do you respond?
choices: Allow publication with no license at all
Require only simple registration to publish
Require a license that officials may deny
Allow only officially approved outlets

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose forcing everyone to recite an official affirmation. How do you respond?
choices: Let people speak or stay silent as they choose
Ask for the affirmation but allow opt-outs
Require most people to recite it
Punish those who refuse

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose banning criticism of public officials. How do you respond?
choices: Let people criticize officials freely
Allow it but punish disrespectful expression
Ban pointed criticism of officials
Punish anyone who speaks against officials

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: A measure would criminalize any expression officials deem disrespectful of the state. How do you respond?
choices: Protect expression, including sharp dissent
Allow speech but punish disrespectful forms of it
Ban pointed criticism of the state
Criminalize dissent against officials

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose banning merchants from advertising their prices and offers. How do you respond?
choices: Let merchants tell people about their goods
Allow advertising but restrict it heavily
Ban most advertising
Allow only officially approved ads

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose that officials license which charities may ask for donations. How do you respond?
choices: Let any honest charity seek support
Allow it but impose heavy registration
Allow only approved charities to collect
Ban charities the state disfavors

12.title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties
12.body: Some propose requiring official pre-approval of online posts. How do you respond?
choices: Let people post without prior approval
Allow posts but pre-screen some of them
Require approval before anyone can post
Allow only approved messages online

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Escalating health costs strain households. Proposals span market competition, subsidies, rationing, and single-payer control. What approach do you adopt?
choices: Enable provider competition and price transparency.
Retain the current system but expand subsidies.
Tighten regulation and restrict permissible treatments.
Establish single-payer control of provision and pricing.

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Qualified nurses could perform more routine care, but scope-of-practice rules reserve it to physicians. What is your decision?
choices: Broaden scope-of-practice for qualified clinicians.
Relax restrictions for a few tasks.
Maintain restrictive scope-of-practice rules.
Have the state allocate all clinical tasks.

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Medicines are markedly cheaper in a neighboring jurisdiction, and some want to bar cross-border purchases. How do you respond?
choices: Permit residents to purchase safe, lower-cost medicine elsewhere.
Allow it under stringent restrictions.
Bar importation of the cheaper medicine.
Grant a public monopoly over the sale of medicine.

12.title: Health Care
12.body: A certificate-of-need rule caps new clinics, and a nurse wants to open one. How do you respond?
choices: Allow new clinics to open and compete.
Permit a limited number of new clinics.
Maintain the cap on new clinics.
Place operation of all clinics with the government.

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Medicine is expensive, and some propose legal price controls. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage competition among sellers to bring prices down
Cap the prices of certain medicines
Impose strict price controls
Put all medicine production under the government

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Some propose requiring everyone to buy one single health plan. How do you respond?
choices: Let people choose the plan that suits them
Require a basic plan while still preserving some choice
Force everyone into one identical plan
Provide coverage through one government plan only

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Some propose making one government office the sole provider of care. How do you respond?
choices: Let many clinics and physicians compete
Allow private care alongside a large public office
Route most care through a single office
Make government the sole provider

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Reformers propose consolidating care under a single public authority to control cost. How do you respond?
choices: Encourage competition among providers, with patients free to choose
Allow private care alongside a dominant public plan
Route all care through a single authority
Make the state the sole provider

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Some propose banning private clinics so that all care is public. How do you respond?
choices: Let private and public care coexist
Allow private care under heavy regulation
Discourage private clinics
Ban private clinics

12.title: Health Care
12.body: Some propose forcing physicians to accept only government-set fees. How do you respond?
choices: Let physicians and patients agree on fees themselves
Set fees but allow some exceptions
Impose one fee schedule on all physicians
Have the state employ all physicians

12.title: Health Care
12.body: A charity clinic provides low-cost care, and the board moves to close it. How do you respond?
choices: Let the clinic keep serving patients
Allow it, but under heavy regulation
Restrict the clinic to protect hospitals
Close the charity clinic
